qs.q: What is the difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM?
qs.a:
Definitions & Key Differences

  • JVM (Java Virtual Machine): The core engine that executes Java bytecode. It is platform-dependent and provides the runtime environment for Java execution.
  • JRE (Java Runtime Environment): The bundle containing the JVM plus core runtime libraries/classes required to run Java applications. It is for running, not developing.
  • JDK (Java Development Kit): The full software development kit containing the JRE plus development tools (javac, debugger, javadoc, jar). It is used to write and compile Java code.
JDK = JRE + Development Tools
JRE = JVM + Core Class Libraries

qs.q: Explain the JVM architecture — class loader, runtime data areas, execution engine.
qs.a:
JVM Architecture Components

1. Class Loader Subsystem: Responsible for Loading, Linking, and Initializing class files. It uses delegation models (Bootstrap, Extension, Application classloaders).
2. Runtime Data Areas (Memory):
  • Method Area: Stores class-level data, constant pool, method metadata, static variables.
  • Heap Area: Stores all objects and instance variables. Shared across threads.
  • Stack Area: Stores local variables, frame data, and method calls. Separate stack per thread.
  • PC Registers: Stores the address of the current execution instruction for each thread.
  • Native Method Stack: Stores native method information.
3. Execution Engine: Executes the bytecode using the Interpreter, JIT Compiler (Just-In-Time to compile hot spots to machine code), and Garbage Collector.

qs.q: What happens when you run a Java program (compilation to execution)?
qs.a:
Step-by-Step Flow

  1. Write Code: Developer writes source code in .java files.
  2. Compilation: The Java Compiler (javac) compiles source code into .class files containing bytecode.
  3. Loading: The JVM Class Loader loads the .class file into memory.
  4. Verification: Bytecode Verifier ensures the code conforms to security rules.
  5. Execution: The Interpreter executes instructions line-by-line. The JIT Compiler compiles highly executed sections into native machine code for direct execution.

qs.q: What is bytecode? Why is Java platform-independent?
qs.a:
Bytecode & Platform Independence

  • Bytecode: An intermediate, highly optimized instruction set generated by compilation. It is not native to any processor but is native to the JVM.
  • Independence: Java compiler creates bytecode (.class) which runs on any system having a platform-specific JRE (JVM). Hence the motto: Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA).

qs.q: What is the difference between stack memory and heap memory?
qs.a:
Stack vs. Heap Memory

FeatureStack MemoryHeap Memory
StorageLocal variables, primitive types, reference pointersAll created objects and instance variables
AccessLIFO (Last-In-First-Out), very fastRandom access, slower due to complex lookup
SizeFixed size, relatively smallDynamic size, much larger
LifespanExists while current method frame is aliveExists as long as referenced, cleaned by GC

qs.q: What is garbage collection? Explain how it works.
qs.a:
Garbage Collection (GC)

Automatic memory management that reclaims heap memory by destroying unreachable objects.

How it works:

1. Marking: GC identifies which objects are in-use and which are dereferenced.
2. Sweeping: GC removes dead objects.
3. Compacting: Moves remaining live objects together to reduce fragmentation.

Generational Heap Structure:

  • Young Generation: Where new objects are allocated (Eden space, Survivor spaces S0, S1). Minor GC runs here frequently.
  • Old (Tenured) Generation: Holds long-surviving objects. Major GC runs here.

qs.q: What is the finalize() method? Why is it deprecated?
qs.a:
finalize() Method

Historically invoked by JVM before garbage collecting an object to release resources.

Why Deprecated:

  • Unpredictable execution timing (no guarantee when or if it will run).
  • Performance overhead.
  • Can cause object resurrection (making object reachable again during finalize).
Alternative: Use try-with-resources and implement AutoCloseable.

qs.q: What are the four pillars of OOP? Explain each.
qs.a:
The Four Pillars of OOP

1. Encapsulation: Bundling data (variables) and methods together, protecting data from unauthorized access using private modifiers and public getter/setter methods.
2. Inheritance: Mechanism where one class acquires the properties and behaviors of a parent class (using extends or implements). Promotes code reusability.
3. Polymorphism: The ability of a message or method to be displayed/behave in more than one form (compile-time overloading, runtime overriding).
4. Abstraction: Hiding internal details and showing only essential features to the user (using abstract classes and interfaces).

qs.q: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?
qs.a:
Overloading vs. Overriding

FeatureMethod OverloadingMethod Overriding
ConceptCompile-time polymorphismRuntime polymorphism
ClassOccurs within the same classOccurs between Parent & Child classes
SignatureMust have different parameter listsMust have the exact same signature
Return TypeCan be differentMust be same or covariant

qs.q: What is runtime polymorphism? Give a code example.
qs.a:
Runtime Polymorphism

A process in which a call to an overridden method is resolved at runtime rather than compile-time based on the object instance.
class Animal {
  void sound() { System.out.println("Animal sound"); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
  void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
}
// Usage:
Animal a = new Dog();
a.sound(); // Output: Bark (dynamic binding)

qs.q: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface?
qs.a:
Abstract Class vs. Interface (Java 8+)

Abstract ClassInterface
Can have instance variables (state).Can only have static final variables (constants).
Can have constructor.Cannot have constructors.
Single inheritance (extends only one).Multiple inheritance (implements multiple).
Can have any access modifiers on methods.Methods are implicitly public.

qs.q: Can an interface have a constructor? Why or why not?
qs.a:
Interface Constructors

No, interfaces cannot have constructors. Why?
  • Interfaces are meant to be pure contracts specifying behavior, not maintaining state.
  • They cannot be instantiated directly, and they cannot have instance variables to initialize.

qs.q: What are default and static methods in interfaces (Java 8+)?
qs.a:
Default & Static Methods

Introduced in Java 8 to allow adding new methods to interfaces without breaking existing implementations.
  • Default Methods (default): Have a method body and can be overridden by classes implementing the interface.
  • Static Methods (static): Have a method body but belong to the interface class itself, cannot be overridden, and are called using the interface name.

qs.q: What is covariant return type in overriding?
qs.a:
Covariant Return Type

Refers to a child class method overriding a parent method and returning a more specific (subclass) type than the parent method's return type.
class Parent {
  Parent get() { return this; }
}
class Child extends Parent {
  @Override
  Child get() { return this; } // Legal in Java 5+
}

qs.q: What is the difference between this and super keywords?
qs.a:
this vs. super

  • this: Points to the current instance of the class. Used to access current class constructors, methods, and fields.
  • super: Points to the immediate parent class instance. Used to access parent class constructors, methods, and overridden fields.

qs.q: What is constructor chaining using this() and super()?
qs.a:
Constructor Chaining

Calling one constructor from another constructor within the same class (using this()) or from the parent class (using super()).
Rules:
1. this() or super() must be the absolute first statement in the constructor.
2. You cannot use both in the same constructor.

qs.q: What a marker interface? Give two examples.
qs.a:
Marker Interface

An empty interface (no fields or methods) used to deliver metadata or 'mark' a class for special treatment by the compiler or JVM.

Examples:

  • java.io.Serializable: Marks class objects as serializable.
  • java.lang.Cloneable: Authorizes the use of Object.clone().

qs.q: Why is String immutable in Java?
qs.a:
Reasons for String Immutability

1. String Pool Cache: Enables reusing string literals, reducing memory usage.
2. Security: Strings are used for database credentials, file paths, and network connections. Mutation could compromise these resources.
3. Thread Safety: Multiple threads can share strings without synchronization synchronization bugs.
4. HashCode Caching: The hashcode of a String is cached upon creation, making hashmap operations fast.

qs.q: What is the String pool (intern pool)? How does it work?
qs.a:
String Pool

A special storage area in Java heap memory where string literals are cached.
  • When you write String s = "hello";, JVM looks for "hello" in the pool. If found, it returns the reference. If not, it creates a new string in the pool.
  • Using new String("hello") bypasses the pool and allocates a new object in the general heap space.

qs.q: What is the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
qs.a:
String comparison

FeatureStringStringBuilderStringBuffer
MutabilityImmutableMutableMutable
Thread-SafeYesNoYes (synchronized)
PerformanceSlower on updatesFastestSlower than Builder

qs.q: What is the difference between == and .equals() for Strings?
qs.a:
== vs. equals()

  • ==: Compares memory references (checks if both pointers point to the exact same object location).
  • .equals(): Compares the actual sequence of characters inside the String (value equivalence).

qs.q: What does String.intern() do?
qs.a:
String.intern()

Explicitly puts a string object in the String pool or returns its reference if it already exists.
String s1 = new String("test");
String s2 = s1.intern(); // s2 points to pool
String s3 = "test";
System.out.println(s2 == s3); // Output: true

qs.q: What is the difference between final, finally, and finalize?
qs.a:
final vs. finally vs. finalize

  • final: Modifier keyword. A final class cannot be inherited, a final method cannot be overridden, and a final variable cannot be reassigned.
  • finally: Block used in exception handling. Guaranteed to execute after try-catch to perform resource cleanup.
  • finalize(): Clean-up method invoked by garbage collector before object destruction.

qs.q: What does the static keyword mean? Can static methods be overridden?
qs.a:
static Keyword

Belongs to the class blueprint rather than any specific object instance.
Can static methods be overridden? No. Method overriding is dynamic dispatch resolved at runtime. Static methods are resolved statically at compile-time (Method Hiding).

qs.q: What is the volatile keyword?
qs.a:
volatile Keyword

Ensures that changes to a variable are always written directly to main memory and read directly from main memory. It guarantees visibility of variable updates across multiple threads without locking, preventing thread caching issues.

qs.q: What is the transient keyword?
qs.a:
transient Keyword

Indicates that a field should not be serialized when saving object state. During deserialization, the transient field is initialized to its default value (e.g. null or 0).

qs.q: What are the four access modifiers and their scope?
qs.a:
Access Modifiers

ModifierClassPackageSubclassGlobal
privateYesNoNoNo
default (no keyword)YesYesNoNo
protectedYesYesYesNo
publicYesYesYesYes

qs.q: What is the instanceof operator?
qs.a:
instanceof Operator

A binary operator used to test if an object is an instance of a specific class, subclass, or interface at runtime.
if (obj instanceof Dog) {
  Dog d = (Dog) obj;
}

qs.q: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?
qs.a:
Checked vs. Unchecked Exceptions

  • Checked Exceptions: Subclasses of Exception (excluding RuntimeException). Checked at compile-time. Code MUST declare or catch them. Examples: IOException, SQLException.
  • Unchecked Exceptions: Subclasses of RuntimeException. Checked at runtime. Do not require compile-time handling. Examples: NullPointerException, ArithmeticException.

qs.q: What is the difference between throw and throws?
qs.a:
throw vs. throws

  • throw: Used to explicitly trigger an exception instance inside a method. Syntax: throw new Exception("Error");
  • throws: Used in method signatures to declare that this method might propagate certain exceptions. Syntax: void test() throws IOException {}

qs.q: What is try-with-resources? When should you use it?
qs.a:
try-with-resources (Java 7+)

A try block that declares one or more resources (objects implementing AutoCloseable or Closeable). These resources are automatically closed in reverse order of initialization when the try block exits.
try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("test.txt"))) {
  System.out.println(br.readLine());
}

qs.q: Can we have a try block without a catch block?
qs.a:
try without catch

Yes, a try block can be compiled without a catch block as long as it has a finally block, or is declared as a try-with-resources statement.

qs.q: What is custom exception? How do you create one?
qs.a:
Custom Exceptions

A user-defined exception class to model specific application errors.
  • Checked exception: Extend java.lang.Exception.
  • Unchecked exception: Extend java.lang.RuntimeException.
class InvalidAgeException extends RuntimeException {
  public InvalidAgeException(String msg) { super(msg); }
}

qs.q: What is the exception hierarchy in Java?
qs.a:
Exception Hierarchy

          Throwable
         /         \
      Error      Exception
                /         \
     IOException   RuntimeException
  • Throwable: Root class of all exceptions and errors.
  • Error: Critical system errors (e.g. OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError). Applications should not attempt to catch.
  • Exception: Application-level conditions that programs should catch.

qs.q: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?
qs.a:
ArrayList vs. LinkedList

ArrayListLinkedList
Backed by dynamic arrays.Backed by doubly linked nodes.
Random access O(1).Sequential access O(n).
Slower inserts/deletes (array shifts).Fast inserts/deletes (pointer changes).
Less memory per element.More memory (stores node links).

qs.q: How does HashMap work internally? What is hashing?
qs.a:
HashMap Internals

Uses an array of Node buckets (Node class contains: int hash, K key, V value, Node next).

Key Actions:

1. put(): Computes key hash code using hashCode(). Determines bucket index via index formula: index = hash & (n - 1).
2. Collision: If multiple keys land in the same bucket, elements are chained in a LinkedList. In Java 8+, if list exceeds threshold (TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8), it turns into a Balanced Tree (Red-Black Tree) to improve lookup from O(n) to O(log n).

qs.q: What is the difference between HashMap and Hashtable?
qs.a:
HashMap vs. Hashtable

HashMapHashtable
Non-synchronized (not thread-safe).Synchronized (thread-safe, slower).
Allows one null key, multiple null values.Does not allow null keys or values.
Fast performance.Legacy class, slow.

qs.q: What is the difference between HashSet, LinkedHashSet, and TreeSet?
qs.a:
Set Comparisons

  • HashSet: Backed by HashMap. Unordered. Fast O(1) operations.
  • LinkedHashSet: Maintains insertion order using a doubly-linked list.
  • TreeSet: Backed by Red-Black Tree. Elements sorted naturally or by Comparator. O(log n) operations.

qs.q: What is the difference between Iterator and ListIterator?
qs.a:
Iterator vs. ListIterator

  • Iterator: Traverse map or lists in forward direction only. Can delete elements.
  • ListIterator: Only for List. Can traverse in both directions (forward/backward) and modify list (add/set elements).

qs.q: What is a fail-fast iterator vs fail-safe iterator?
qs.a:
Fail-Fast vs. Fail-Safe Iterators

  • Fail-Fast: Works on original collection. Throws ConcurrentModificationException if collection is modified structurally during iteration (using modCount). Examples: ArrayList, HashMap iterators.
  • Fail-Safe (Weakly Consistent): Works on a copy of the collection. Does not throw exceptions if modified during iteration. Examples: CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap.

qs.q: What is Comparable vs Comparator?
qs.a:
Comparable vs. Comparator

  • Comparable: Interface implementing compareTo(obj) to define natural sorting of the class. Modifies target class directly.
  • Comparator: Separate helper class implementing compare(obj1, obj2) to define custom sorting rules. Does not modify original class.

qs.q: What is the difference between poll() and remove() in Queue?
qs.a:
Queue Method Comparison

Both retrieve and remove the head element of the queue.
  • poll(): Returns null if the queue is empty.
  • remove(): Throws NoSuchElementException if the queue is empty.

qs.q: When would you use a PriorityQueue?
qs.a:
PriorityQueue

Used when elements must be processed based on their natural priority or custom ordering rather than standard FIFO.
Example: Job scheduling systems where high priority tasks run first.

qs.q: What are generics and why are they used?
qs.a:
Generics

Introduced in Java 5 to enable type-safe programming.

Benefits:

  • Type safety (enforces correct data types at compile time).
  • Eliminates runtime typecasting and class cast exceptions.
  • Allows writing generic algorithms that apply to multiple types.

qs.q: What is type erasure in generics?
qs.a:
Type Erasure

The process by which the Java compiler strips generic type parameters at compile-time and replaces them with their bounds (or Object) to maintain backwards compatibility with older, raw non-generic code. No generic info exists at JVM runtime.

qs.q: What is the difference between List<?>, List<Object>, and List<T>?
qs.a:
List Type Differences

  • List: List of unknown type (Wildcard). Read-only; you cannot add elements to it (except null) because compiler cannot guarantee the list type.
  • List

qs.q: What is an upper-bounded wildcard (extends) vs lower-bounded wildcard (super)?
qs.a:
Upper bounds vs. Lower bounds

  • Upper-Bounded (? extends T): Restricts elements to type T and subclasses. Read operations are safe; write operations are restricted (useful for producers).
  • Lower-Bounded (? super T): Restricts elements to type T and superclasses. Safe to write elements of type T; read operations yield only type Object (useful for consumers).

qs.q: What is a functional interface? Give examples from java.util.function.
qs.a:
Functional Interface

An interface containing exactly one abstract method (annotated with @FunctionalInterface). Can contain multiple default or static methods.

Examples from java.util.function:

  • Predicate: Takes T, returns boolean (test(T t)).
  • Function: Takes T, returns R (apply(T t)).
  • Consumer: Takes T, returns void (accept(T t)).
  • Supplier: Takes nothing, returns T (get()).

qs.q: What are lambda expressions? Write an example.
qs.a:
Lambda Expressions

An anonymous block of code (function) used to implement a functional interface in a concise way.
// Traditional anonymous class:
Runnable r1 = new Runnable() {
  public void run() { System.out.println("Hi"); }
};
// Lambda expression:
Runnable r2 = () -> System.out.println("Hi");

qs.q: What are the common Stream API operations (map, filter, reduce, collect)?
qs.a:
Stream Operations

  • filter(): Intermediate operation. Selects elements matching a predicate.
  • map(): Intermediate operation. Transforms each element using a function.
  • reduce(): Terminal operation. Aggregates stream elements into a single value.
  • collect(): Terminal operation. Converts streams to lists, sets, etc.

qs.q: What is the difference between map() and flatMap()?
qs.a:
map() vs. flatMap()

  • map(): One-to-one transformation. Transforms elements but keeps the structural nested nesting (e.g. stream of lists remains a stream of lists).
  • flatMap(): One-to-many transformation. Flattens nested collections (e.g. flattens Stream<List<String>> into a unified Stream<String>).

qs.q: What is the Optional class and how does it prevent NullPointerException?
qs.a:
Optional Class (Java 8)

A container wrapper object which may or may not contain a non-null value. It helps design clean APIs that avoid returning null.
Optional name = Optional.ofNullable(getName());
String result = name.orElse("Default Name"); // avoids NPE

qs.q: What are method references? What are the four types?
qs.a:
Method References

Shorthand syntax for lambdas that call existing methods. Syntax uses ::.

Four Types:

1. Static method reference: Math::max
2. Instance method reference on specific object: System.out::println
3. Instance method reference of arbitrary object type: String::toUpperCase
4. Constructor reference: ArrayList::new

qs.q: What is the difference between intermediate and terminal stream operations?
qs.a:
Intermediate vs. Terminal Operations

  • Intermediate: Returns another Stream. Lazily evaluated (doesn't run until terminal operation runs). Examples: filter(), map().
  • Terminal: Performs aggregation or side-effects and returns a non-stream type. Triggers execution of the stream pipeline. Examples: collect(), forEach(), count().

qs.q: What is the difference between Thread and Runnable?
qs.a:
Thread vs. Runnable

  • Thread: Class representing a thread of execution. You must subclass it and override run(). Cons: limits class inheritance.
  • Runnable: Interface defining run() method. Better practice as you can implement it while extending other parent classes.

qs.q: What are the thread states in Java?
qs.a:
Thread Lifecycle States

1. NEW: Created but not yet started (before start()).
2. RUNNABLE: Ready to execute (waiting in thread pool scheduler queue).
3. BLOCKED: Waiting to acquire a monitor lock.
4. WAITING: Waiting indefinitely for notification (e.g. via wait()).
5. TIMED_WAITING: Waiting for specified duration (e.g. sleep(ms)).
6. TERMINATED: Finished executing run code.

qs.q: What is synchronization? What problem does it solve?
qs.a:
Synchronization

Controlling multi-thread access to a shared resource. Prevents race conditions where concurrent writes corrupt data state.

qs.q: What is the difference between synchronized method and synchronized block?
qs.a:
Synchronized Method vs. Block

  • Synchronized Method: Locks the entire method. Synchronizes on the current instance (this) or class object.
  • Synchronized Block: Locks only specified statements. Allows choosing target lock object, maximizing performance by narrowing block scope.

qs.q: What is the difference between wait() and sleep()?
qs.a:
wait() vs. sleep()

Featurewait()sleep()
ClassObject classThread class
LocksReleases monitor lockRetains lock ownership
Wake upNeeds notify()/notifyAll()Awakens automatically after timeout

qs.q: What is a deadlock? How can you prevent it?
qs.a:
Deadlock

A scenario where thread A holds lock 1 and waits for lock 2, while thread B holds lock 2 and waits for lock 1. Both wait forever.

Prevention:

  • Acquire locks in consistent global order.
  • Avoid locking nested resources.
  • Use timeouts (like ReentrantLock.tryLock()).

qs.q: What is the difference between Runnable and Callable?
qs.a:
Runnable vs. Callable

  • Runnable: Cannot return method results. Cannot throw checked exceptions (uses run()).
  • Callable (Java 5): Returns a generic result value. Can throw checked exceptions (uses call()).

qs.q: What is a thread pool (ExecutorService)?
qs.a:
Thread Pools

A managed cache pool of reusable worker threads. Avoids resource overhead of creating/destroying threads constantly.
Created using Executors factory methods (e.g. newFixedThreadPool(n)).

qs.q: What is serialization and deserialization? What is serialVersionUID?
qs.a:
Serialization

Converting an object state into a byte stream to write to files/databases or transmit over network. Deserialization is restoring object state from that byte stream.

serialVersionUID:

A unique version identifier associated with serialized data to verify that the sender and receiver classes have matching compatible versions.

qs.q: What is shallow copy vs deep copy?
qs.a:
Shallow vs. Deep Copy

  • Shallow Copy: Copies the top-level object fields. References inside are shared between copy and original object.
  • Deep Copy: Recursively copies all objects referenced, resulting in completely independent objects.

qs.q: What is autoboxing and unboxing?
qs.a:
Autoboxing & Unboxing

  • Autoboxing: Compiler automatically converting primitive types to corresponding wrapper classes (e.g., int to Integer).
  • Unboxing: Converting wrapper object to primitive (e.g., Integer to int).

qs.q: What are varargs? Give an example.
qs.a:
Varargs (Variable Arguments)

Enables a method to accept zero or more arguments of a specified type using ... syntax.
void printAll(String... names) {
  for(String s : names) System.out.println(s);
}

qs.q: What is an enum? Can an enum have a constructor and methods?
qs.a:
Enum

A special class type defining a fixed set of constants.
Yes, enums can contain instance fields, constructors (implicitly private), and methods.

qs.q: What is the difference between DDL, DML, DCL, and TCL? Give examples of each.
qs.a:
SQL Sublanguages

  • DDL (Data Definition Language): Defines/modifies database structures. Examples: CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE.
  • DML (Data Manipulation Language): Manages data within objects. Examples: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.
  • DCL (Data Control Language): Controls privileges and permissions. Examples: GRANT, REVOKE.
  • TCL (Transaction Control Language): Manages transactions. Examples: COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT.

qs.q: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
qs.a:
DELETE vs. TRUNCATE vs. DROP

FeatureDELETETRUNCATEDROP
TypeDMLDDLDDL
ActionRemoves specified rows (where clause allowed)Removes all rows from tableDeletes table structure and all data
RollbackYes (writes to log)No (depends on DB engines, usually auto-commits)No
SpeedSlowerFastFastest

qs.q: What is a PRIMARY KEY vs a UNIQUE constraint?
qs.a:
PRIMARY KEY vs. UNIQUE

  • PRIMARY KEY: Uniquely identifies each row. Does not allow NULL values. Only one per table. Automatically creates a clustered index.
  • UNIQUE constraint: Ensures all values in a column are unique. Allows NULL values (usually one null depending on database system). Multiple UNIQUE constraints allowed per table.

qs.q: What is a FOREIGN KEY? What does ON DELETE CASCADE do?
qs.a:
Foreign Keys & Cascade

  • FOREIGN KEY: A column or group of columns that establishes a link between data in two tables (enforcing referential integrity).
  • ON DELETE CASCADE: If a row in the parent table is deleted, all matching rows in the child table containing that foreign key are automatically deleted.

qs.q: What is a composite key?
qs.a:
Composite Key

A primary key composed of two or more columns to uniquely identify a row in a table. Used when a single column is insufficient to guarantee uniqueness.

qs.q: What is the difference between CHAR and VARCHAR?
qs.a:
CHAR vs. VARCHAR

  • CHAR: Fixed-length character data type. Pads spaces to fill maximum length if input is shorter. Fast access.
  • VARCHAR: Variable-length character data type. Stores only characters entered plus a length byte. More storage-efficient.

qs.q: What is the difference between DATETIME and TIMESTAMP?
qs.a:
DATETIME vs. TIMESTAMP

  • DATETIME: Stores date and time independently of timezone (ranges 1000 to 9999).
  • TIMESTAMP: Stores date and time converted to UTC time. Auto-converts to client timezone when fetched (ranges 1970 to 2038).

qs.q: What are the different types of JOINs? Explain each with an example.
qs.a:
SQL JOIN Types

  • INNER JOIN: Returns rows that have matching values in both tables.
  • LEFT JOIN: Returns all rows from left table, plus matched rows from right (fills NULLs if no match).
  • RIGHT JOIN: Returns all rows from right table, plus matched rows from left.
  • FULL JOIN: Returns rows when there is a match in either table.

qs.q: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and OUTER JOIN?
qs.a:
INNER vs. OUTER JOIN

  • INNER JOIN: Drops rows that do not have matching partners in both tables.
  • OUTER JOIN (Left/Right/Full): Retains non-matching rows and pads missing columns with NULL values.

qs.q: What is a SELF JOIN? When is it useful?
qs.a:
Self Joins

A join where a table is joined with itself. Requires assigning alias names (e.g. T1, T2).
Use case: Querying organizational hierarchy (finding Employee and their matching Manager inside the same Employee table).

qs.q: What is the difference between CROSS JOIN and FULL OUTER JOIN?
qs.a:
CROSS vs. FULL JOIN

  • CROSS JOIN: Returns the Cartesian product (every row of table A matched with every row of table B). No ON clause.
  • FULL OUTER JOIN: Matches tables based on join condition, keeping unmatched rows from both tables.

qs.q: What is a NATURAL JOIN? What are its risks?
qs.a:
Natural Join

Joins tables automatically based on columns that have the exact same name and data type in both tables.
Risks: Very dangerous. If a new column with same name is added to either table, query logic silently changes and breaks without warning.

qs.q: Write a query to find employees who earn more than their manager.
qs.a:
Employee-Manager Salary Comparison

SELECT e.name AS Employee
FROM Employee e
JOIN Employee m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;

qs.q: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
qs.a:
WHERE vs. HAVING

  • WHERE: Filters rows BEFORE grouping is performed. Cannot evaluate aggregate functions.
  • HAVING: Filters group results AFTER GROUP BY. Can evaluate aggregates like SUM() or COUNT().

qs.q: What is the difference between GROUP BY and ORDER BY?
qs.a:
Grouping vs. Sorting

  • GROUP BY: Collapses rows sharing same values into aggregate summary rows.
  • ORDER BY: Sorts the final output records (ascending or descending).

qs.q: What are aggregate functions? Name all of them.
qs.a:
Aggregate Functions

Functions that compute a single summary value from a set of input column values.
Common list: SUM(), AVG(), COUNT(), MIN(), MAX().

qs.q: What is the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column_name)?
qs.a:
COUNT Comparisons

  • COUNT(*): Counts all rows in the group, including duplicate values and NULL rows.
  • COUNT(column_name): Counts only non-NULL entries in that column.

qs.q: How does SQL handle NULL in aggregate functions?
qs.a:
NULL in Aggregates

All aggregate functions (except COUNT(*)) silently ignore NULL values during computation. If all values are NULL, the function returns NULL.

qs.q: What is the difference between IS NULL and = NULL?
qs.a:
= NULL vs. IS NULL

  • IS NULL: The correct syntax to check for empty values.
  • = NULL: Incorrect. NULL indicates unknown state, so comparing value = NULL evaluates to UNKNOWN (never returns true).

qs.q: What is COALESCE? Give a use case.
qs.a:
COALESCE() Function

Returns the first non-null value in the argument list.
SELECT COALESCE(phone, email, 'No Contact') FROM users;

qs.q: What is the CASE statement in SQL? Give an example.
qs.a:
SQL CASE Statement

Conditional statement to return specific values based on conditions.
SELECT name,
CASE
  WHEN score >= 90 THEN 'A'
  ELSE 'B'
END AS Grade
FROM students;

qs.q: What is the difference between EXISTS and IN? Which is faster?
qs.a:
EXISTS vs. IN

  • IN: Scans the entire subquery result list. Good for small subquery sets.
  • EXISTS: Short-circuits (stops execution once first match is found). Faster for large tables and when using correlated subqueries.

qs.q: What is the difference between ANY and ALL operators?
qs.a:
ANY vs. ALL

  • ANY: Evaluates to TRUE if any value returned by the subquery meets the condition.
  • ALL: Evaluates to TRUE only if all values returned by the subquery meet the condition.

qs.q: What is a subquery? What are the types of subqueries?
qs.a:
Subqueries

A query nested inside another query (such as SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE).

Types:

  • Single-row: Returns one value. Used with =, >.
  • Multi-row: Returns list of values. Used with IN, ANY, ALL.
  • Correlated: Subquery depends on the outer query row.

qs.q: What is a correlated subquery? How is it different from a non-correlated subquery?
qs.a:
Correlated vs. Non-Correlated

  • Non-Correlated: Runs independently of outer query. Executed once.
  • Correlated: References columns of the outer query. Runs repeatedly (once for each row processed by the outer query).

qs.q: What is a CTE (Common Table Expression)? Write the syntax.
qs.a:
CTEs

A temporary named result set available only during execution of a single query. Improves readability over subqueries.
WITH SalesCTE AS (
  SELECT dept_id, SUM(amount) AS total
  FROM sales GROUP BY dept_id
)
SELECT * FROM SalesCTE WHERE total > 10000;

qs.q: What is a recursive CTE? Give an example (e.g., hierarchy tree).
qs.a:
Recursive CTE

A CTE that references itself to query hierarchical data.
WITH RECURSIVE EmpHierarchy AS (
  SELECT id, name, manager_id FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id 
  FROM employees e JOIN EmpHierarchy h ON e.manager_id = h.id
) SELECT * FROM EmpHierarchy;

qs.q: What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
qs.a:
UNION vs. UNION ALL

  • UNION: Merges results of two queries, removing duplicates. Slower due to duplicate sorting.
  • UNION ALL: Merges results of two queries, retaining duplicate rows. Much faster.

qs.q: What is INTERSECT and EXCEPT (MINUS)?
qs.a:
INTERSECT & EXCEPT

  • INTERSECT: Returns only rows shared in common by both queries.
  • EXCEPT (MINUS in Oracle): Returns rows from first query not present in second query.

qs.q: What are window functions? How are they different from aggregate functions?
qs.a:
Window Functions

Perform calculations across a set of table rows related to the current row without collapsing them into a single row.
Syntax: Uses the OVER() clause.

qs.q: What is the difference between ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), and DENSE_RANK()?
qs.a:
Row Numbering & Ranking

For values [100, 100, 200, 300]:
  • ROW_NUMBER(): Assigns unique linear numbers: [1, 2, 3, 4].
  • RANK(): Assigns matching rank, skipping numbers: [1, 1, 3, 4].
  • DENSE_RANK(): Assigns matching rank, no gaps: [1, 1, 2, 3].

qs.q: What is PARTITION BY in a window function?
qs.a:
PARTITION BY

Divides rows into groups/partitions (e.g. by Department) to calculate window metrics independently within each group.

qs.q: What is LEAD() and LAG()? Give a use case.
qs.a:
LEAD & LAG

  • LAG(): Accesses data from a previous row at a specified offset.
  • LEAD(): Accesses data from a subsequent row at a specified offset.
Use case: Calculating year-over-year sales growth.

qs.q: What is NTILE()?
qs.a:
NTILE()

Divides ordered rows in a partition into a specified number of ranked groups (e.g. NTILE(4) creates quartiles).

qs.q: Write a query to find the top 3 salaries per department using window functions.
qs.a:
Top Salaries per Department

SELECT dept_id, name, salary FROM (
  SELECT dept_id, name, salary,
  DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) as rk
  FROM employees
) t WHERE rk <= 3;

qs.q: What is an index? How does it speed up queries?
qs.a:
Database Indexes

A data structure (usually B-Tree) that stores pointers to data rows to quickly locate records without scanning the entire table (Full Table Scan).

qs.q: What is the difference between a clustered and a non-clustered index?
qs.a:
Clustered vs. Non-Clustered

  • Clustered: Sorts and stores physical data rows in index order. Only one per table (typically Primary Key).
  • Non-Clustered: Separate structure containing index columns and row pointers. Multiple allowed.

qs.q: When should you NOT add an index?
qs.a:
When to Avoid Indexes

  • On small tables (FTS is faster).
  • On tables with frequent writes/updates (updates are slowed down by index updates).
  • On columns with low selectivity (e.g. Gender flags).

qs.q: What is an execution plan and how do you read it?
qs.a:
Execution Plans

The execution steps chosen by the query optimizer (using EXPLAIN). Look for indices used vs. scans (Index Scan vs Table Scan) to identify bottlenecks.

qs.q: How do you optimize a slow-running SQL query?
qs.a:
Optimization Checklist

1. Use EXPLAIN to analyze access path.
2. Add indices on WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY columns.
3. Avoid SELECT * (fetch only required columns).
4. Use JOINs instead of correlated subqueries.
5. Avoid wildcards at beginning of patterns (e.g. LIKE '%term').

qs.q: What is a covering index?
qs.a:
Covering Index

An index that contains all columns requested by the SELECT query. The database engine can satisfy the query entirely from the index without reading table blocks.

qs.q: What is a VIEW? What are its advantages?
qs.a:
Views

A virtual table based on the result set of an SQL query.
Advantages: Simplifies complex queries, provides secure column access, maintains logical interface consistency.

qs.q: What is the difference between a view and a materialized view?
qs.a:
View vs. Materialized View

  • Standard View: Virtual table, executes query dynamically every time it is referenced.
  • Materialized View: Physical table, saves query output. Needs periodic refresh, very fast for heavy aggregates.

qs.q: What is a stored procedure? Write a simple example.
qs.a:
Stored Procedure

A group of SQL statements saved and executed on the database server side.
CREATE PROCEDURE GetDeptSalary(IN d_id INT, OUT total DECIMAL)
BEGIN
  SELECT SUM(salary) INTO total FROM employees WHERE dept_id = d_id;
END;

qs.q: What is the difference between a stored procedure and a function?
qs.a:
Procedure vs. Function

  • Function: Must return a single value. Can be called inline in SELECT (e.g. SELECT func()). Cannot perform DML updates.
  • Stored Procedure: Does not require returning values. Cannot be called inside SELECT. Can run updates and transaction controls.

qs.q: What is a TRIGGER? When would you use one?
qs.a:
Triggers

An automated database action that fires when DML events (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) occur on a table.
Use case: Maintaining audit tables or enforcing data entry validations.

qs.q: What are transactions? Explain the ACID properties.
qs.a:
ACID Properties

  • Atomicity: All statements in transaction succeed, or all fail together.
  • Consistency: Database transitions only from one valid state to another.
  • Isolation: Transactions execute independently without cross-leakage.
  • Durability: Changes are permanently saved in database even during power outages.

qs.q: What is the difference between COMMIT and ROLLBACK?
qs.a:
COMMIT vs. ROLLBACK

  • COMMIT: Permanently saves transaction updates.
  • ROLLBACK: Undoes all changes back to the start of the transaction.

qs.q: What is a SAVEPOINT?
qs.a:
SAVEPOINT

Creates checkpoint references inside a transaction to allow partial rollback of statements without canceling the entire transaction.

qs.q: What are the SQL isolation levels? (Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable)
qs.a:
Transaction Isolation Levels

Isolation LevelDirty ReadNon-Repeatable ReadPhantom Read
Read UncommittedYesYesYes
Read CommittedNoYesYes
Repeatable ReadNoNoYes
SerializableNoNoNo

qs.q: What is a dirty read, phantom read, and non-repeatable read?
qs.a:
Concurrency Anomalies

  • Dirty Read: Reading uncommitted changes from another transaction (which might get rolled back later).
  • Non-Repeatable Read: Reading the same row twice yields different column values because another transaction updated it.
  • Phantom Read: Re-executing a range query yields new insert rows matching criteria because another transaction added them.

qs.q: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?
qs.a:
Locking Strategies

  • Optimistic: Assumes conflicts are rare. Uses version checks at update time. No database lock held during read.
  • Pessimistic: Assumes conflicts are common. Locks the records immediately on read (e.g. SELECT ... FOR UPDATE).

qs.q: What is a deadlock in SQL? How is it resolved?
qs.a:
SQL Deadlocks

Two transactions wait for locks held by each other. The database engine automatically detects deadlocks, cancels one transaction (victim), and rolls it back.

qs.q: What is normalization? Explain 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF with examples.
qs.a:
Database Normalization

Structuring tables to minimize data redundancy and dependency issues.
  • 1NF (First Normal Form): Values in each cell must be atomic (no multi-valued lists).
  • 2NF (Second Normal Form): Must be in 1NF. All non-key attributes must be fully functionally dependent on the entire primary key (no partial dependencies).
  • 3NF (Third Normal Form): Must be in 2NF. No transitive dependencies (non-key columns cannot depend on other non-key columns).

qs.q: What is BCNF?
qs.a:
BCNF (Boyce-Codd Normal Form)

A slightly stronger version of 3NF. Requires that for any functional dependency A -> B, the determinant A must be a super key.

qs.q: What is denormalization? When is it appropriate?
qs.a:
Denormalization

Purposely introducing redundancy (joining tables back) to speed up complex queries by reducing expensive JOIN operations. Appropriate in high-read Data Warehouses (OLAP).

qs.q: Write a query to find the second highest salary.
qs.a:
Second Highest Salary

SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees
WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);

qs.q: Write a query to find duplicate records in a table.
qs.a:
Find Duplicates

SELECT email, COUNT(*)
FROM users
GROUP BY email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

qs.q: Write a query to delete duplicate rows while keeping one.
qs.a:
Delete Duplicates

DELETE u1 FROM users u1
JOIN users u2 ON u1.email = u2.email
WHERE u1.id > u2.id; -- deletes higher IDs, keeping lowest

qs.q: Write a query to find the nth highest salary.
qs.a:
Nth Highest Salary

SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM employees e1
WHERE (n - 1) = (
  SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT salary) FROM employees e2
  WHERE e2.salary > e1.salary
);

qs.q: Write a query to pivot rows into columns.
qs.a:
Pivot Query

SELECT year,
  SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Jan' THEN sales ELSE 0 END) as Jan_Sales,
  SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Feb' THEN sales ELSE 0 END) as Feb_Sales
FROM monthly_sales GROUP BY year;

qs.q: What is JDBC and what are its main components?
qs.a:
JDBC Fundamentals

Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) is a Java API used to connect and execute queries with databases.

Key Components:

  • DriverManager: Manages database driver registrations.
  • Connection: Represents session interface with the database.
  • Statement: Container for submitting SQL text query plans.
  • ResultSet: Holds output data rows returned by database.
  • SQLException: Class handling query errors.

qs.q: What are the steps to connect to a database using JDBC?
qs.a:
JDBC Connection Steps

  1. Import packages: import java.sql.*;
  2. Load and register driver class (optional in JDBC 4.0+): Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
  3. Establish connection: Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, password);
  4. Create Statement: Statement stmt = conn.createStatement();
  5. Execute query: ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM users");
  6. Process results and Close resources inside try-with-resources.

qs.q: What is the JDBC URL format? Write the MySQL URL format.
qs.a:
JDBC URL Format

A connection string that points database drivers to target servers.
Syntax: jdbc:<subprotocol>:<subname>
MySQL Syntax: jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/db_name?useSSL=false&serverTimezone=UTC

qs.q: What is the role of Class.forName() in JDBC (older vs newer approach)?
qs.a:
Class.forName()

Used to dynamically load and register the database driver class into JVM memory.
  • Older: Mandatory. Executing Class.forName() initialized the static blocks in driver classes which registered themselves with DriverManager.
  • Newer (JDBC 4.0+): Optional. Drivers matching standard Service Provider Interface (SPI) are auto-loaded from JAR classpath class manifest lookups.

qs.q: What is the difference between Statement, PreparedStatement, and CallableStatement?
qs.a:
Statement Comparisons

  • Statement: Compiles and runs static SQL queries dynamically. Vulnerable to SQL injection.
  • PreparedStatement: Compiles query structures once, allowing parameter placeholders (?). Prevents SQL injection, faster execution.
  • CallableStatement: Extends PreparedStatement to call database stored procedures, managing IN/OUT parameters.

qs.q: Why is PreparedStatement preferred over Statement?
qs.a:
Why Use PreparedStatement?

1. Precompilation: DB compiles SQL outline once, caching execution plan for repeat parameters.
2. Security: Parameters are treated strictly as literal values, stripping SQL command structures to prevent SQL Injection.
3. Readability: Code is cleaner compared to messy string concatenation.

qs.q: What are the execute(), executeQuery(), and executeUpdate() methods?
qs.a:
JDBC Execution Methods

  • executeQuery(): Used for SELECT statements. Returns a single ResultSet object.
  • executeUpdate(): Used for INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE. Returns integer count of affected rows.
  • execute(): General method. Returns true if first result is a ResultSet (SELECT), false if it is an update count or yields no results.

qs.q: What are the types of ResultSet (scroll sensitivity + updatability)?
qs.a:
ResultSet Types

  • Scroll Sensitivity:
    • TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY: Default. Move cursor forward only.
    • TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE: Bidirectional scrolling, does not reflect active database changes made by others.
    • TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE: Bidirectional, reflects live database updates.
  • Updatability:
    • CONCUR_READ_ONLY: Cannot modify data through ResultSet.
    • CONCUR_UPDATABLE: Allows updating database tables through ResultSet cursor methods.

qs.q: What is DatabaseMetaData? What information does it provide?
qs.a:
DatabaseMetaData

Metadata details about the database server (capabilities, limits, configuration).
Information: database product name, version, driver name, supported transaction isolation levels, list of tables, database columns.

qs.q: What is ResultSetMetaData?
qs.a:
ResultSetMetaData

Metadata details about the columns inside a returned ResultSet.
Information: number of columns, column names, column data types, table origin names.

qs.q: What is connection pooling? Why is it critical in production?
qs.a:
Connection Pooling

A pool of pre-created active database connections managed by a broker (e.g. HikariCP).
Importance: Creating database TCP sockets and authenticating takes considerable time. Pooling enables clients to instantly acquire, use, and release connections back, avoiding latency spikes and resource exhaustion.

qs.q: What is a DataSource? How does it differ from DriverManager?
qs.a:
DataSource vs. DriverManager

  • DriverManager: Traditional approach. Generates new connections directly using driver classes on every request. Not configurable via JNDI.
  • DataSource: Modern enterprise standard interface. Wraps database connection details, supports pooling, caching, distributed transactions, and can be registered in JNDI directories.

qs.q: How do you perform a batch update in JDBC?
qs.a:
Batching in JDBC

Enables sending multiple SQL statements in a single network round-trip to database.
conn.setAutoCommit(false);
PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement("INSERT INTO log VALUES(?)");
for(String m : logs) {
  ps.setString(1, m);
  ps.addBatch();
}
int[] results = ps.executeBatch();
conn.commit();

qs.q: How do you manage transactions manually in JDBC?
qs.a:
Manual Transactions

  1. Turn off auto-commit: conn.setAutoCommit(false);
  2. Execute statements.
  3. On success: conn.commit();
  4. On exception: conn.rollback();

qs.q: What is auto-commit mode and when would you turn it off?
qs.a:
Auto-Commit Mode

Default JDBC behavior where every statement executes as an independent transaction and commits immediately.
When to turn off: When grouping multiple statements as a single atomic unit of work (e.g. money transfer debit/credit) or doing batch updates.

qs.q: How do you call a stored procedure using CallableStatement?
qs.a:
Calling Stored Procedures

CallableStatement cs = conn.prepareCall("{call getSalary(?, ?)}");
cs.setInt(1, 101); // IN parameter
cs.registerOutParameter(2, Types.DECIMAL); // OUT parameter
cs.execute();
double salary = cs.getDouble(2);

qs.q: What is a RowSet? What are the types?
qs.a:
RowSet

A wrapper around ResultSet which is easier to pass around. Can be disconnected from the database.

Types:

  • JdbcRowSet: Connected wrapper.
  • CachedRowSet: Disconnected container. Can cache data in memory and sync back.
  • WebRowSet: Can read/write XML data representations.

qs.q: How do you handle a BLOB/CLOB using JDBC?
qs.a:
BLOB / CLOB

  • BLOB (Binary Large Object): For files, images. Read using getBlob(), write using setBinaryStream().
  • CLOB (Character Large Object): For large text docs. Read using getClob(), write using setCharacterStream().

qs.q: How do you prevent SQL injection in JDBC?
qs.a:
Preventing SQL Injection

Always use PreparedStatement parameterized inputs instead of raw string concatenations. Never build SQL by gluing variables directly: "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='" + input + "'".

qs.q: What is the difference between JDBC 3.0 and JDBC 4.0?
qs.a:
JDBC 3.0 vs. JDBC 4.0

  • Auto-loading drivers: JDBC 4.0 loads drivers from classpath automatically (no Class.forName needed).
  • Annotations: Supported XML metadata configurations can be simplified using annotations.
  • SQLXML support: Adds native types for handling XML files.

qs.q: What is a Servlet and what is its complete lifecycle?
qs.a:
Servlet Definition & Lifecycle

A Java class that extends dynamic server processing capabilities, running on a web container to handle HTTP requests.

Lifecycle Phases:

  1. Loading & Instantiation: Web container reads config files and instantiates servlet instance.
  2. Initialization: Container runs init(ServletConfig) once.
  3. Service execution: Runs service(req, res) on every incoming request (dispatches to doGet, doPost, etc.). Executed in separate threads.
  4. Destruction: Container runs destroy() before removing the servlet from memory.

qs.q: What is the difference between GenericServlet and HttpServlet?
qs.a:
GenericServlet vs. HttpServlet

  • GenericServlet: Protocol-independent. Implements basic lifecycle. Must override service() method.
  • HttpServlet: Protocol-specific (HTTP). Extends GenericServlet. Automates dispatching to methods like doGet(), doPost(), etc.

qs.q: What is the difference between init(), service(), and destroy()?
qs.a:
Servlet Lifecycle Methods

  • init(): Runs once to setup config variables. Called during initialization.
  • service(): Called repeatedly for every single HTTP request. Decides GET/POST types.
  • destroy(): Runs once when container shuts down, releasing file sockets or DB connections.

qs.q: What is web.xml and what does it configure?
qs.a:
web.xml Deployment Descriptor

The configuration file for servlet applications. Defines web assets mappings: servlet class paths, routing URLs (servlet-mapping), security constraints, filters, session timeouts, and listeners.

qs.q: What is the @WebServlet annotation?
qs.a:
@WebServlet (Servlet 3.0+)

Annotation to define a class as a Servlet and map URL paths directly in java classes, eliminating the need to declare servlets in web.xml.
@WebServlet("/users")
public class UserServlet extends HttpServlet { ... }

qs.q: What is the difference between ServletConfig and ServletContext?
qs.a:
ServletConfig vs. ServletContext

ServletConfigServletContext
One instance per Servlet definition.One instance per Web Application context.
Used to pass init parameters to a specific servlet.Used to share parameters globally across all servlets in the app.

qs.q: What are init-param and context-param?
qs.a:
Init vs Context Parameters

  • init-param: Key-value configurations defined within a specific servlet in web.xml. Accessed using getServletConfig().getInitParameter().
  • context-param: Global parameters defined outside any servlet tags. Accessed using getServletContext().getInitParameter().

qs.q: What is a welcome file list?
qs.a:
Welcome File List

Configuration in web.xml that defines files (like index.html, home.jsp) to load automatically if a user requests the root directory URL path.

qs.q: What is the difference between doGet() and doPost()?
qs.a:
doGet vs. doPost

  • doGet(): Appends parameters to the URL query string (visible, limited size, cached, bookmarkable). Used to fetch data.
  • doPost(): Sends data in HTTP body (hidden, unlimited size, not cached). Used to modify/create resource state.

qs.q: What is the difference between the HTTP GET and POST methods?
qs.a:
HTTP GET vs. POST

GET is designed for safe, idempotent queries without side-effects. POST is designed for modifications, database updates, and non-idempotent operations.

qs.q: What is RequestDispatcher? What is the difference between forward() and include()?
qs.a:
RequestDispatcher

Used to delegate requests to other web components (servlets, JSPs, HTML).
  • forward(): Transfers request processing entirely. Target component takes over control, response is committed by target. Url on client browser doesn't change.
  • include(): Temporarily includes content from another resource, then returns execution back to original servlet to finish response.

qs.q: What is the difference between sendRedirect() and forward()?
qs.a:
forward() vs. sendRedirect()

  • HTTP Requests
  • URL Browser
    • Featureforward()sendRedirect()
    Routing locationInternal server-side forwardingClient-side redirect (returns 302 code)
    1 request/response cycle2 separate request cycles
    Remains original servlet URLChanges to redirected URL location

qs.q: How do you read request parameters in a Servlet?
qs.a:
Reading Parameters

Use the HttpServletRequest methods:
  • getParameter("name"): Returns single parameter string.
  • getParameterValues("hobbies"): Returns string array (checkboxes).

qs.q: How do you handle file uploads in a Servlet (Multipart)?
qs.a:
File Uploads (Servlet 3.0+)

Annotate servlet with @MultipartConfig and fetch file parts using request APIs:
@MultipartConfig
public class Upload extends HttpServlet {
  protected void doPost(request, response) {
    Part filePart = request.getPart("file");
    filePart.write("output.txt");
  }
}

qs.q: What is a Servlet filter? Give a real use case.
qs.a:
Servlet Filters

A component that intercepts requests and responses before they reach the servlet to perform pre/post-processing tasks.
Real use cases: Logging request details, verifying user authentication tokens, converting string formats (encoding), and CORS header management.

qs.q: What is a Servlet listener? Name some listener interfaces.
qs.a:
Servlet Listeners

Objects that monitor life events of the web application (context load, session creation, request lifecycle changes).
Examples: ServletContextListener, HttpSessionListener, ServletRequestListener.

qs.q: How do you handle thread safety in a Servlet?
qs.a:
Thread Safety in Servlets

Servlet containers run a single shared servlet instance, invoking service() in multiple parallel threads. To stay thread-safe:
  • Avoid class instance variables (state).
  • Keep variables confined inside local method scopes (method variables are allocated on thread stack).
  • Synchronize shared objects explicitly if necessary (though not recommended as it reduces performance).

qs.q: What is SingleThreadModel and why was it deprecated?
qs.a:
SingleThreadModel

A deprecated legacy interface that forced containers to instantiate a pool of servlets or run requests sequentially (single thread per servlet). It did not truly solve concurrency bugs, consumed too much memory, and hurt performance.

qs.q: What is session management? Why is it needed in HTTP?
qs.a:
Session Management

A method to maintain client state across multiple page requests. Needed because HTTP is a stateless protocol; each request is treated as independent and holds no history of previous actions.

qs.q: What are the four techniques of session management?
qs.a:
Session Tracking Techniques

1. Cookies: Key-value text stored on client browser, sent in headers automatically.
2. HttpSession API: Key identifier (JSESSIONID) stored in client cookie, mapping to memory storage on the server.
3. URL Rewriting: Appending session ID directly into all hyperlinks: url?jsessionid=123.
4. Hidden Form Fields: Using invisible input fields inside HTML forms: <input type='hidden' name='session' value='123'>.

qs.q: What is HttpSession? Key methods?
qs.a:
HttpSession API

Server-side mechanism for user session persistence.
Key methods: setAttribute(key, val), getAttribute(key), removeAttribute(key), invalidate() (destroys session).

qs.q: What is the difference between HttpSession, Cookies, and URL Rewriting?
qs.a:
Session Comparison

  • Cookies: Stores data entirely on client side. Vulnerable to modification and can be disabled by users.
  • HttpSession: Stores data securely on server side. Client only holds the unique session ID.
  • URL Rewriting: Works even if client browser cookies are disabled, but exposes session parameters in browser URL.

qs.q: What is session timeout and how do you configure it?
qs.a:
Session Timeout

The period of inactivity after which the server automatically invalidates a session.
Configuration: Defined in web.xml using minutes:
<session-config>
  <session-timeout>30</session-timeout>
</session-config>

qs.q: What is JSESSIONID?
qs.a:
JSESSIONID

A unique session identifier token generated automatically by servlet containers to associate incoming requests with their matching HttpSession stored in server memory.

qs.q: What is JSP and how is it different from Servlet?
qs.a:
JSP vs. Servlet

  • JSP (JavaServer Pages): Document-centric (looks like HTML with Java tags embedded). Used mainly for view rendering. Auto-compiles to a servlet behind the scenes.
  • Servlet: Java-centric code. Used mainly for controller logic, parsing forms, database operations, routing.

qs.q: What is the JSP page lifecycle?
qs.a:
JSP Lifecycle

During request, container reads JSP and compiles it into a Java Servlet class:
Translation -> Compilation (.class) -> Instantiation -> _jspInit() -> _jspService() -> _jspDestroy().

qs.q: What are JSP directives? Name all three and their purpose.
qs.a:
JSP Directives

Instructions that direct the container on how to process the page. Syntax: <%@ directive ... %>.
1. page: Defines page-specific settings (imports, error pages).
2. include: Inserts file content during translation phase (static include).
3. taglib: Declares custom tag libraries (JSTL) used on page.

qs.q: What are the 9 JSP implicit objects?
qs.a:
JSP Implicit Objects

Predefined Java objects available inside JSP scriptlets automatically:
1. request (HttpServletRequest)
2. response (HttpServletResponse)
3. out (JspWriter)
4. session (HttpSession)
5. application (ServletContext)
6. config (ServletConfig)
7. pageContext (PageContext)
8. page (this servlet class instance)
9. exception (Throwable - only inside error pages)

qs.q: What is the difference between JSP scriptlet, expression, and declaration?
qs.a:
JSP Scripting Elements

  • Scriptlets (<% ... %>): Embeds executable Java statements into the service method.
  • Expressions (<%= ... %>): Evaluates expressions, prints values directly to response. No semicolons.
  • Declarations (<%! ... %>): Declares variables or helper methods outside the service method (as class-level members).

qs.q: What is Expression Language (EL)? Why is it preferred over scriptlets?
qs.a:
Expression Language (EL)

A simplified expression syntax (e.g. ${user.name}) to access data models.
Why preferred: Enables building JSPs without embedding ugly Java scriptlet code, improving design isolation and readability.

qs.q: What is JSTL? Name key JSTL tag libraries.
qs.a:
JSTL (JSP Standard Tag Library)

A standard library of tags for common JSP design logic (loops, conditions, database access).
Libraries: Core (c:if, c:forEach), Formatting (fmt), SQL (sql), XML (x).

qs.q: What is the difference between the include directive and <jsp:include> action?
qs.a:
JSP Includes

  • include directive (<%@ include %>): Static inclusion. Happens during translation phase. Merges file contents first, compiles once. Best for static headers.
  • include action (<jsp:include>): Dynamic inclusion. Executes target page at runtime, inserts result output. Best for dynamic page layouts.

qs.q: What is the difference between <jsp:forward> and sendRedirect()?
qs.a:
JSP Forward vs. Redirect

  • <jsp:forward>: Server-side forward using RequestDispatcher. URL remains unchanged.
  • sendRedirect(): Client-side redirect. Involves browser reloading another URL.

qs.q: What are JSP custom tags?
qs.a:
JSP Custom Tags

User-defined elements that execute customized Java handlers to replace Java scriptlets, keeping views clean. Formats extend SimpleTagSupport classes.

qs.q: What is the difference between session scope, request scope, and application scope?
qs.a:
JSP Scope Scopes

  • request: Data persists only for the duration of the current request/response cycle.
  • session: Data persists across multiple user page views until session invalidates.
  • application: Data is shared globally across all users of the entire web application.

qs.q: How do you pass data from Servlet to JSP?
qs.a:
Servlet to JSP Data Sharing

Set attributes in request or session scopes in the servlet, then forward request to JSP:
// In Servlet:
request.setAttribute("info", "success");
request.getRequestDispatcher("view.jsp").forward(request, response);
// In JSP:
String msg = (String) request.getAttribute("info"); // or ${info}

qs.q: What is the MVC pattern in Servlet+JSP? Explain roles of each layer.
qs.a:
MVC Pattern

  • Model: Plain Java objects (POJO, Entities) representing application state, database data, business logic.
  • View: JSP files rendering HTML/CSS UI interface containing data passed from Controller.
  • Controller: Servlet intercepts incoming client requests, manages models, chooses which JSPs to route output results.

qs.q: What is the page directive in JSP? Key attributes?
qs.a:
JSP Page Directive

Defines properties for the entire JSP file.
Key attributes: import, contentType, errorPage, isErrorPage, session (boolean).

qs.q: How do you handle exceptions in JSP (errorPage attribute)?
qs.a:
JSP Error Pages

Configure error bindings using page directives:
1. In source page: <%@ page errorPage="error.jsp" %>
2. In error handler page: <%@ page isErrorPage="true" %> (this unlocks the exception implicit object).

qs.q: What is the difference between out.print() and out.println() in JSP?
qs.a:
out.print vs. out.println

Both write characters to the client output writer. println() appends a platform-dependent line separator (newline) to the output stream, but in HTML this is rendered as standard white space rather than a line break (<br>).

qs.q: What is CORS? How would you handle it in a Servlet filter?
qs.a:
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)

A browser security mechanism that blocks scripts running on origin A from accessing resources hosted at origin B. Handle by injecting CORS headers in filter responses:
res.setHeader("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*");
res.setHeader("Access-Control-Allow-Methods", "GET, POST, OPTIONS");

qs.q: What is Hibernate? What problem does ORM solve over plain JDBC?
qs.a:
Hibernate & ORM

Hibernate is an Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) framework for Java. It maps Java domain objects to database tables.

Problems solved over JDBC:

  • Eliminates boilerplate SQL mappings.
  • Provides object caching (First/Second level).
  • Ensures database independence (dialects translate HQL to specific SQL dialects).
  • Automates transaction management and connection pooling integrations.

qs.q: What is the difference between JDBC and Hibernate?
qs.a:
JDBC vs. Hibernate

JDBCHibernate
Low-level database connectivity API.High-level ORM framework built on JDBC.
Developer writes raw, platform-specific SQL queries.Developer works with objects; Hibernate generates SQL.
No built-in caching mechanism.Contains automatic caching strategies.

qs.q: What is hibernate.cfg.xml? What are the essential properties?
qs.a:
hibernate.cfg.xml

The main configuration file setting up DB connections, dialects, and mappings.
Essential properties: connection.url, connection.username, connection.password, dialect (translates queries), show_sql (logs SQL to console), hbm2ddl.auto (auto schema updates).

qs.q: What is an entity in Hibernate? What are the mandatory annotations?
qs.a:
Hibernate Entities

A Java class mapped to a database table. It must meet POJO standards (default constructor, getter/setters).
Mandatory annotations: @Entity (marks class as entity) and @Id (marks primary key field).

qs.q: What is @Entity, @Table, @Id, @Column?
qs.a:
Core JPA/Hibernate Annotations

  • @Entity: Registers class as mapped entity.
  • @Table: Specifies table name (optional, defaults to class name).
  • @Id: Declares primary key.
  • @Column: Maps attribute to specific database column name/limits.

qs.q: What are GenerationType strategies? (AUTO, IDENTITY, SEQUENCE, TABLE)
qs.a:
Primary Key Generation Strategies

  • IDENTITY: Database auto-increment column (e.g. MySQL AUTO_INCREMENT). Disables Hibernate batch inserts because ID requires insert immediate.
  • SEQUENCE: Uses database Sequence generators. Highly efficient, supports batching.
  • TABLE: Uses a separate database table to track key counters. Poor performance, rarely used.
  • AUTO: Delegates strategy choice to Hibernate based on database support.

qs.q: What is the difference between openSession() and getCurrentSession()?
qs.a:
openSession() vs. getCurrentSession()

  • openSession(): Always creates a new, independent Session. Must close manually.
  • getCurrentSession(): Binds session lifecycle to current transaction. Automatically flushes and closes when transaction ends.

qs.q: What is the SessionFactory and when is it built?
qs.a:
SessionFactory

A thread-safe, heavyweight factory object holding compiled mappings. Built during application startup. Usually one SessionFactory per database source.

qs.q: What are the states of a Hibernate entity? (Transient, Persistent, Detached, Removed)
qs.a:
Entity Lifecycle States

  • Transient: Object created in memory but not associated with Hibernate Session or database (no ID).
  • Persistent: Object monitored by Session, representing a database row. Changes are auto-synchronized to DB during flush.
  • Detached: Session is closed or cleared; object exists but is no longer monitored.
  • Removed: Scheduled for deletion on transaction commit.

qs.q: What is the difference between save(), persist(), saveOrUpdate(), and merge()?
qs.a:
Lifecycle Methods

  • persist(): JPA standard. Makes a transient instance persistent. Returns void. Does not guarantee immediate ID assign.
  • save(): Hibernate specific. Returns generated identifier immediately.
  • saveOrUpdate(): Saves if transient, updates if detached.
  • merge(): Copies state of detached object to a persistent instance, returning the persistent reference.

qs.q: What is the difference between get() and load()?
qs.a:
get() vs. load()

get()load()
Fetches data immediately from database.Returns a lazy proxy object immediately. DB query runs only on field access.
Returns null if ID doesn't exist.Throws ObjectNotFoundException if ID doesn't exist.

qs.q: What is a dirty check in Hibernate? When does it trigger an UPDATE?
qs.a:
Dirty Checking

Automatic update mechanism. At flush time, Hibernate compares current object field values with snapshots taken during load. If updates are found, it generates and executes SQL UPDATE statements automatically.

qs.q: What is the difference between evict(), clear(), and close() on a Session?
qs.a:
Releasing Session State

  • evict(entity): Detaches a single entity instance from Session cache.
  • clear(): Detaches all entities, clearing the entire session.
  • close(): Terminates session instance entirely, releasing database resources.

qs.q: What is HQL? How is it different from SQL?
qs.a:
HQL vs. SQL

  • HQL (Hibernate Query Language): Object-oriented query language targeting Java Class names and entity properties.
  • SQL: Database-specific language targeting actual Table names and columns.

qs.q: What is a named query (@NamedQuery)? Advantage?
qs.a:
Named Queries

Static queries defined using annotations on entity classes.
Advantage: Pre-compiled at boot time, catching syntax errors early. Improves security and query organization.

qs.q: What is the Criteria API (JPA Criteria)?
qs.a:
Criteria API

A programmatic, type-safe API used to build dynamic queries using Java object methods, preventing runtime SQL syntax errors.

qs.q: What is the difference between HQL and Criteria API?
qs.a:
HQL vs. Criteria API

HQL is written as textual queries (easier for simple commands), whereas Criteria API uses object-oriented code interfaces (better for compiling complex, dynamic query forms).

qs.q: How do you use native SQL queries in Hibernate?
qs.a:
Native SQL in Hibernate

Use the createNativeQuery() method:
List users = session.createNativeQuery("SELECT * FROM tbl_user").list();

qs.q: What is pagination in Hibernate? (setFirstResult / setMaxResults)
qs.a:
Pagination

Configure offsets directly using method parameters:
Query q = session.createQuery("from User");
q.setFirstResult(10); // offset index
q.setMaxResults(5);   // limit size

qs.q: What is lazy loading vs eager loading?
qs.a:
Lazy vs. Eager

  • Lazy Loading: Child entities/collections are not fetched from DB until explicitly accessed in code. Saves database resources.
  • Eager Loading: Fetches parent and children together in one single query. Can cause heavy memory usage.

qs.q: What is FetchType.LAZY vs FetchType.EAGER?
qs.a:
JPA Fetch Types

Mapped using relation parameters:
@ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY). In JPA, @OneToMany defaults to LAZY, and @ManyToOne defaults to EAGER.

qs.q: What is the N+1 select problem? How do you solve it?
qs.a:
N+1 Select Problem

A performance bottleneck where fetching 1 parent entity requires N additional database queries to fetch matching child collections individually (usually during loops).
Solutions: Use JOIN FETCH queries, define @BatchSize on collection mappings, or configure EntityGraph profiles.

qs.q: What is JOIN FETCH in HQL?
qs.a:
JOIN FETCH

Tells Hibernate to fetch associated collections in the same initial query using an SQL JOIN, avoiding lazy-load N+1 select triggers.

qs.q: What is batch fetching? How is it configured?
qs.a:
Batch Fetching

An optimization fetching child collections in batches (e.g. 10 at a time) using IN clauses instead of individual queries.
Configuration: Mapped using @BatchSize(size = 10) on collection declarations.

qs.q: How do you map a @OneToMany relationship?
qs.a:
@OneToMany Mapping

@Entity
public class Department {
  @OneToMany(mappedBy = "department")
  private List employees;
}

qs.q: What is the difference between @OneToMany and @ManyToOne?
qs.a:
One-to-Many vs. Many-to-One

They represent the two sides of a bidirectional relationship. @ManyToOne represents the child side pointing to a parent and holds the actual foreign key column (owning side).

qs.q: How do you map a @ManyToMany relationship (join table)?
qs.a:
@ManyToMany Mapping

Requires a third intermediary join table:
@ManyToMany
@JoinTable(
  name = "student_course",
  joinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "student_id"),
  inverseJoinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "course_id")
)
private Set courses;

qs.q: What is @OneToOne? Unidirectional vs bidirectional?
qs.a:
@OneToOne

One-to-One relation mapping. In unidirectional mapping, only one entity has a foreign key to the other. In bidirectional, the child entity uses mappedBy pointing back to the owning entity.

qs.q: What is the mappedBy attribute? When is it required?
qs.a:
mappedBy Attribute

Tells Hibernate that this side is the inverse (non-owning) relationship partner and matching foreign key attributes are mapped on the target entity class. Required in bidirectional relationships.

qs.q: What is @JoinColumn?
qs.a:
@JoinColumn

Defines the actual physical foreign key column name to create inside the database table.

qs.q: What is @Embeddable and @Embedded?
qs.a:
Embedded Components

Used to store modular property sets (e.g. Address containing zip, city) in the same database table as the parent entity.
  • @Embeddable: Placed on component class.
  • @Embedded: Placed on parent class attribute.

qs.q: What is @Transient in Hibernate?
qs.a:
@Transient

Declares that a field is purely temporary and should not be mapped to any database column.

qs.q: What are CascadeType options? What does CascadeType.ALL include?
qs.a:
Cascade Types

Propagates changes made on a parent object down to child objects automatically.
Options: PERSIST, MERGE, REMOVE, REFRESH, DETACH.
CascadeType.ALL groups all of the above options.

qs.q: What is orphanRemoval?
qs.a:
orphanRemoval

If set to true (orphanRemoval = true), removing a child entity reference from a parent collection will automatically delete that child row from the database.

qs.q: What is first-level cache in Hibernate? Can you disable it?
qs.a:
First-Level Cache

Session-level, transactional cache. Enabled by default for all sessions and **cannot be disabled**. Keeps track of loaded entities within the current active session boundary.

qs.q: What is second-level cache? How do you configure it (EhCache)?
qs.a:
Second-Level Cache

Heavyweight SessionFactory-level cache. Shared across all user sessions. Remains disabled by default.
Configuration: Configured by setting cache providers in config files (e.g., org.hibernate.cache.ehcache.EhCacheRegionFactory) and mapping entities using @Cacheable annotations.

qs.q: What is a query cache?
qs.a:
Query Cache

Caches the actual query result IDs for exact parameters. Works in combination with second-level cache.

qs.q: What is the difference between read-only and read-write second-level cache strategies?
qs.a:
Cache Concurrency Strategies

  • read-only: Good for static reference tables that never change. Simple, fast.
  • read-write: Used if data is updated. Uses soft-locks to guarantee transaction isolation during concurrent database modifications.

qs.q: What is inheritance mapping in Hibernate? Name the three strategies.
qs.a:
Inheritance Mapping

Mapping subclass OOP models to relational tables.
1. SINGLE_TABLE (default)
2. JOINED
3. TABLE_PER_CLASS

qs.q: Explain SINGLE_TABLE vs JOINED vs TABLE_PER_CLASS with trade-offs.
qs.a:
Inheritance Strategies Comparison

  • SINGLE_TABLE: Saves all classes in one table using a discriminator column. Fast, but columns of subclasses must allow NULLs.
  • JOINED: Subclasses have separate tables linked via join keys. Clean relational structure, but slow due to JOINS.
  • TABLE_PER_CLASS: Subclasses have fully duplicated tables. No null columns, but query queries across parent types require slow UNION commands.

qs.q: What is optimistic locking? How is @Version used?
qs.a:
Optimistic Locking

Locks data conceptually without database locks. Maps a version column (@Version). If a write operation conflicts with an updated version, Hibernate aborts and throws an OptimisticLockException.

qs.q: What is pessimistic locking in Hibernate?
qs.a:
Pessimistic Locking

Uses database-level locks directly (e.g., FOR UPDATE) to block other readers/writers until current transaction ends.

qs.q: What is connection pooling in Hibernate? (C3P0, HikariCP)
qs.a:
Hibernate Connection Pools

Configured using properties in hibernate.cfg.xml (e.g. hibernate.hikari.maximumPoolSize) to enable and manage database socket pooling libraries.

qs.q: What does the hbm2ddl.auto (ddl-auto) property do? Values?
qs.a:
hbm2ddl.auto

Controls schema generation at startup.
Values: create (drops and creates tables), create-drop (creates at startup, drops at shutdown), update (modifies table schemas dynamically), validate (checks structures but makes no modifications).

qs.q: What is batch processing in Hibernate and why is flush+clear needed?
qs.a:
Hibernate Batch Processing

When updating thousands of records, Hibernate accumulates instances in the first-level cache. To avoid OutOfMemoryError, we must commit batch changes and clean the cache at intervals:
if (i % 50 == 0) {
  session.flush();
  session.clear();
}

qs.q: What is the difference between Hibernate and JPA?
qs.a:
JPA vs. Hibernate

  • JPA: Java Persistence API is a specification (a set of interfaces/rules).
  • Hibernate: A concrete provider framework that implements the JPA specification.

qs.q: What is Spring Data JPA and how does it relate to Hibernate?
qs.a:
Spring Data JPA

An abstraction layer built on top of JPA providers (like Hibernate). It eliminates DAO implementation boilerplate using repository interfaces (e.g., JpaRepository).

qs.q: What is the Spring Framework and what are its core modules?
qs.a:
Spring Framework

An open-source enterprise Java framework providing comprehensive infrastructure support.
Core modules: Spring Core (IoC, DI), Spring AOP (aspects), Spring JDBC/ORM (data access), Spring Web (MVC, REST), and Spring Test.

qs.q: What is Inversion of Control (IoC)?
qs.a:
Inversion of Control (IoC)

A design principle in which control over object creation, configuration, and lifecycles is transferred from the program code to an external container or framework (the Spring IoC container).

qs.q: What is Dependency Injection (DI)? What problem does it solve?
qs.a:
Dependency Injection (DI)

A design pattern executing IoC. Objects do not instantiate their dependencies directly; instead, dependencies are supplied/injected by the container (e.g. constructor/setter).
Problems solved: Decouples classes, simplifies unit testing (allows mock injections), and increases code reusability.

qs.q: What are the types of Dependency Injection in Spring?
qs.a:
Types of DI

1. Constructor Injection: Dependencies are passed via class constructor.
2. Setter Injection: Dependencies are passed via public setter methods.
3. Field Injection: Dependencies are injected directly into class attributes using reflection (annotated with @Autowired).

qs.q: What is the difference between constructor injection and setter injection? When to prefer each?
qs.a:
Constructor vs. Setter Injection

  • Constructor Injection: Best for mandatory dependencies. Guarantees object immutability, prevents null-pointer risks, and simplifies unit tests. Highly recommended.
  • Setter Injection: Best for optional or mutable dependencies. Allows changing dependencies dynamically later.

qs.q: What is the Spring IoC container?
qs.a:
Spring IoC Container

The core manager of the Spring framework. It instantiates, configures, wires, and manages the entire lifecycle of application objects (Spring Beans).

qs.q: What is the difference between BeanFactory and ApplicationContext?
qs.a:
BeanFactory vs. ApplicationContext

BeanFactoryApplicationContext
Basic IoC container.Advanced enterprise-level IoC container.
Loads beans lazily (on demand).Pre-instantiates singleton beans at startup (eager).
Minimal resources, good for lightweight devices.Adds internationalization (i18n), AOP integration, and application event listeners.

qs.q: What is a Spring Bean?
qs.a:
Spring Beans

Objects instantiated, managed, and wired together by the Spring IoC container.

qs.q: What is the complete Spring Bean lifecycle?
qs.a:
