Bean Lifecycle Steps

1. Instantiate Class
2. Populate properties (Dependency Injection)
3. Call aware interfaces (e.g. BeanNameAware)
4. BeanPostProcessor pre-initialization (postProcessBeforeInitialization)
5. Custom Initialization (@PostConstruct, afterPropertiesSet, custom init-method)
6. BeanPostProcessor post-initialization (postProcessAfterInitialization)
7. Bean is ready for use
8. Destruction (@PreDestroy, destroy(), custom destroy-method)

qs.q: What is init-method and destroy-method in Spring?
qs.a:
init-method and destroy-method

Declarative initialization and cleanup hooks configured inside bean metadata:
@Bean(initMethod = "setup", destroyMethod = "cleanup")

qs.q: What are @PostConstruct and @PreDestroy?
qs.a:
Lifecycle Annotations

  • @PostConstruct: Applied to methods to execute custom initialization code after dependency injections complete.
  • @PreDestroy: Applied to methods to execute cleanup before the bean is destroyed inside context.

qs.q: What are the bean scopes in Spring? (singleton, prototype, request, session, application)
qs.a:
Bean Scopes

  • singleton (default): Only one shared instance created per IoC container.
  • prototype: Creates a new bean instance every time it is requested.
  • request: Creates one instance per HTTP request lifecycle (web apps).
  • session: Creates one instance per HTTP session lifecycle (web apps).
  • application: Shared global instance per ServletContext.

qs.q: What is the difference between singleton and prototype scope in detail?
qs.a:
Singleton vs. Prototype

  • Singleton: Cached in memory, shared across all injections. Container controls entire lifecycle.
  • Prototype: Instantiated on demand. Container injects dependencies but hands control to client (destruction lifecycle hooks are not called automatically).

qs.q: What is bean wiring?
qs.a:
Bean Wiring

The process of injecting dependencies and linking bean references within the IoC container (can be configured via XML, Java Config, or Autowired annotations).

qs.q: What is @Component? How is it different from @Bean?
qs.a:
@Component vs. @Bean

  • @Component: Class-level annotation. Spring automatically detects and registers classes using component scanning. Used for classes you write.
  • @Bean: Method-level annotation inside @Configuration classes. Tells Spring to register the returned object instance as a bean. Used for third-party libraries.

qs.q: What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller?
qs.a:
Component Specializations

They are structural stereotypes for auto-detection:
  • @Component: Generic component flag.
  • @Service: Indicates business service logic.
  • @Repository: Indicates DAO database persistence layer. Translates database exceptions automatically.
  • @Controller: Indicates MVC UI request handler.

qs.q: What is @Autowired? Where can it be applied?
qs.a:
@Autowired

Instructs Spring to perform automatic dependency injection. Can be applied to constructors, setter methods, or fields.

qs.q: What is @Qualifier? When do you need it?
qs.a:
@Qualifier

Used when multiple beans of the same class type exist in the container. Helps target a specific bean to resolve injection ambiguity:
@Autowired
@Qualifier("sqlEngine")
private Engine engine;

qs.q: What is @Primary?
qs.a:
@Primary

Gives default priority to a specific bean when multiple implementation candidates exist for injection.

qs.q: What is @Value? How do you inject a property from application.properties?
qs.a:
@Value Property Injection

Injects configuration properties directly into bean fields:
@Value("${server.port}")
private int port;

qs.q: What is @Configuration?
qs.a:
@Configuration

Registers a class as a source of bean definitions containing @Bean methods.

qs.q: What is @ComponentScan?
qs.a:
@ComponentScan

Tells Spring to scan packages for classes annotated with stereotypes (Component, Service, etc.) and register them in the context.

qs.q: What is the difference between @Autowired and @Inject?
qs.a:
@Autowired vs. @Inject

  • @Autowired: Spring-specific annotation.
  • @Inject: Standard Java CDI annotation (JSR-330). Requires additional dependencies.

qs.q: What is @Lazy?
qs.a:
@Lazy

Instructs the container to initialize a bean only when requested, rather than eagerly at startup.

qs.q: What is Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP)?
qs.a:
Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP)

A programming paradigm that complements OOP by isolating cross-cutting concerns (e.g. logging, transactions, security) from business logic using modular structures called Aspects.

qs.q: What is an Aspect, Advice, Pointcut, and JoinPoint?
qs.a:
AOP Terminology

  • Aspect: Module containing cross-cutting logic.
  • JoinPoint: Any candidate location in code (e.g., method execution) where advice can be applied.
  • Advice: Action logic to run at a JoinPoint (e.g. log before execution).
  • Pointcut: Expression targeting specific JoinPoints to execute Advice.

qs.q: What are the types of Advice in Spring AOP?
qs.a:
AOP Advice Types

Defined using annotations: @Before, @After, @AfterReturning, @AfterThrowing, @Around.

qs.q: What is the @Before, @After, @AfterReturning, @AfterThrowing, and @Around advice?
qs.a:
Advice Annotations

  • @Before: Executes before method starts.
  • @After: Executes after method completes (regardless of outcome).
  • @AfterReturning: Executes only after successful method execution.
  • @AfterThrowing: Executes only if method throws exceptions.
  • @Around: Wraps method execution, managing parameters, returns, and execution control.

qs.q: What is the difference between Spring AOP and AspectJ AOP?
qs.a:
Spring AOP vs. AspectJ

  • Spring AOP: Proxy-based, runtime compilation. Only supports method-execution joinpoints. Lightweight.
  • AspectJ: Compiler/weaver-based, compile/post-compile weaving. Supports field access, constructor, and method execution. Heavyweight, high performance.

qs.q: What is a proxy in Spring AOP? (JDK dynamic vs CGLIB)
qs.a:
Spring AOP Proxies

Spring wraps classes inside proxy objects to apply aspects.
  • JDK Dynamic Proxy: Default for classes implementing interfaces. Uses Java reflection APIs.
  • CGLIB Proxy: Used if a class doesn't implement interfaces. Generates subclass definitions dynamically.

qs.q: Give a real use case where AOP is useful (logging, security, transactions).
qs.a:
AOP Use Cases

Example: Declarative Transaction management (@Transactional). The transaction aspect opens a transaction before method start, commits on success, and rolls back on exception automatically, keeping code clean.

qs.q: What is Spring MVC? What is the DispatcherServlet?
qs.a:
Spring MVC

Model-View-Controller framework built around a central front controller servlet, the DispatcherServlet, which coordinates incoming request routing and response rendering.

qs.q: What is the request flow in Spring MVC?
qs.a:
Request Handling Flow

1. Client sends request to DispatcherServlet.
2. DispatcherServlet consults HandlerMapping to find target Controller.
3. Controller processes request, returning a ModelAndView object.
4. DispatcherServlet consults ViewResolver to locate actual View file.
5. DispatcherServlet dispatches data to View for rendering, returning output to Client.

qs.q: What is @RequestMapping? What are its attributes?
qs.a:
@RequestMapping

Maps URL paths to controller classes or methods.
Attributes: value (path), method (GET/POST), headers, params, produces/consumes.

qs.q: What is the difference between @GetMapping/@PostMapping and @RequestMapping?
qs.a:
GET/POST Annotations

@GetMapping and @PostMapping are specialized shortcut annotations representing @RequestMapping(method = RequestMethod.GET) etc.

qs.q: What is @PathVariable vs @RequestParam?
qs.a:
Path vs Query Parameters

  • @PathVariable: Extracts variables embedded directly in URL path segment: /users/{id}.
  • @RequestParam: Extracts standard HTTP query parameters: /users?id=123.

qs.q: What is @RequestBody and @ResponseBody?
qs.a:
Body Mapping

  • @RequestBody: Deserializes incoming HTTP request body JSON into a Java object.
  • @ResponseBody: Serializes Java return objects into HTTP response JSON.

qs.q: What is the difference between @RestController and @Controller?
qs.a:
@Controller vs. @RestController

  • @Controller: Used to return traditional Views (HTML pages).
  • @RestController: Groups @Controller and @ResponseBody. Returns serialized data models (JSON/XML) directly. Best for REST APIs.

qs.q: What is ModelAndView?
qs.a:
ModelAndView

A container wrapping both target View name strings and data model attributes returned by controller methods.

qs.q: What is a ViewResolver?
qs.a:
ViewResolver

A component that translates abstract view names returned by controllers (e.g. "home") into physical view paths (e.g. "/WEB-INF/views/home.jsp").

qs.q: What is @ExceptionHandler and @ControllerAdvice?
qs.a:
Global Exception Handling

  • @ExceptionHandler: Declares an exception handling method inside a specific controller.
  • @ControllerAdvice: Declares a global exception handler intercepting errors across all controllers.

qs.q: What is @ResponseStatus?
qs.a:
@ResponseStatus

Binds an explicit HTTP status code (e.g. HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND) to a controller method or custom exception class.

qs.q: What is Spring Data JPA?
qs.a:
Spring Data JPA

Abstraction framework eliminating standard JPA repository boilerplate code using interfaces that auto-generate database operations.

qs.q: What is JpaRepository? What methods does it provide?
qs.a:
JpaRepository

Interface providing CRUD, sorting, and pagination methods (e.g. save(), findAll(), deleteById()).

qs.q: What are derived query methods in Spring Data JPA?
qs.a:
Derived Query Methods

Database queries compiled automatically from interface method names: findByEmailAddress(String email) translates into SQL WHERE clauses.

qs.q: What is @Transactional? What is transaction propagation?
qs.a:
@Transactional & Propagation

Defines declarative transactional boundaries on classes or methods. Propagation defines transaction boundaries behavior if a method is called inside an existing transaction.

qs.q: What are the propagation types? (REQUIRED, REQUIRES_NEW, NESTED, etc.)
qs.a:
Propagation Types

  • REQUIRED (default): Joins existing transaction or creates a new one.
  • REQUIRES_NEW: Always creates a new transaction, suspending any active ones.
  • NESTED: Executes inside a nested transaction using savepoints.

qs.q: What is rollbackFor in @Transactional?
qs.a:
rollbackFor

Configures transactional rollbacks for specific checked exceptions. By default, Spring only rolls back transactions for unchecked/runtime exceptions.

qs.q: What is the difference between @Transactional on a class vs method?
qs.a:
Transactional Scope

Applying to a class makes all its public methods transactional, while applying to a method restricts transactional boundaries to that specific method only.

qs.q: What is Spring Boot? How is it different from Spring Framework?
qs.a:
Spring Boot vs. Spring Framework

  • Spring Framework: Lightweight dependency core requiring extensive XML or Java configs, web setups, and deployment descriptors.
  • Spring Boot: Extension of Spring providing pre-configured starters, auto-configurations, and embedded servers to build production-ready applications instantly.

qs.q: What is auto-configuration in Spring Boot?
qs.a:
Auto-Configuration

A mechanism where Spring Boot automatically configures beans based on JAR libraries found in the classpath (e.g. auto-configures DataSource beans if MySQL connector is present).

qs.q: What is a Spring Boot starter?
qs.a:
Starters

Dependency descriptors grouping common libraries together under a single entry (e.g., spring-boot-starter-web includes Tomcat, Spring Web, and JSON mapping packages).

qs.q: What is @SpringBootApplication? What annotations does it combine?
qs.a:
@SpringBootApplication

The bootstrap entry point annotation. Combines three annotations:
1. @SpringBootConfiguration
2. @EnableAutoConfiguration
3. @ComponentScan

qs.q: What is the embedded server in Spring Boot? How does it work?
qs.a:
Embedded Server

Bundles the application container (Apache Tomcat/Jetty) inside the executable JAR. Eliminates the need to deploy WAR files to external application servers.

qs.q: What is application.properties vs application.yml?
qs.a:
Configuration Formats

  • properties: Flat key-value format (server.port=8080).
  • yml: Hierarchical, nested structure using indentation (more readable for large configurations).

qs.q: What is the Spring Boot actuator?
qs.a:
Actuator

A library providing production-ready monitoring endpoints to check app health, metrics, configurations, thread dumps, and logs.

qs.q: What is CommandLineRunner?
qs.a:
CommandLineRunner

Interface whose run() method executes automatically once the Spring Boot application starts up.

qs.q: What is Spring Security basics?
qs.a:
Spring Security

A customizable authentication and access-control framework protecting Spring applications.

qs.q: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
qs.a:
Authentication vs. Authorization

  • Authentication: Verifying user identity (Who are you?).
  • Authorization: Verifying resource permissions (What are you allowed to do?).

qs.q: What is a security filter chain in Spring Security?
qs.a:
Security Filter Chain

A chain of filter components that intercept requests to parse credentials, verify security tokens, evaluate URL rules, and authenticate endpoints.

qs.q: What is JWT? How does the token flow work?
qs.a:
JWT (JSON Web Token)

A secure, stateless JSON token signed cryptographically. The client logs in, receives a token, and includes it in the Authorization: Bearer header of subsequent requests. The server validates the token signature without database queries.

qs.q: What is Basic Auth vs Token Auth?
qs.a:
Basic vs. Token Auth

  • Basic Auth: Sends base64-encoded username/password in headers of every request. Stateless, but credentials are exposed.
  • Token Auth: Sends a temporary cryptographically secure key token instead of credentials.

qs.q: What is @PreAuthorize?
qs.a:
@PreAuthorize

A method-level annotation enforcing security rules using SpEL expressions before the method executes:
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")

qs.q: What is OAuth2 at a conceptual level?
qs.a:
OAuth2

An industry-standard authorization framework enabling third-party applications to obtain limited access to user resources using token exchanges without exposing user passwords.

qs.q: What is REST? What are the six REST architectural constraints?
qs.a:
REST (Representational State Transfer)

An architectural style for designing networked applications using stateless, client-server communications.

Six Constraints:

1. Uniform Interface
2. Client-Server
3. Stateless
4. Cacheable
5. Layered System
6. Code on Demand (optional)

qs.q: What is the difference between REST and SOAP?
qs.a:
REST vs. SOAP

RESTSOAP
Architectural style using simple HTTP protocols.Strict XML-based protocol.
Supports JSON, XML, Plain text, HTML formats.Supports XML format only.
Stateless, flexible, lightweight.Heavyweight, has built-in security specifications (WS-Security).

qs.q: What are the HTTP methods and when to use each? (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS)
qs.a:
HTTP Methods

  • GET: Fetches resources.
  • POST: Creates new resources.
  • PUT: Replaces existing resources entirely.
  • PATCH: Modifies resources partially.
  • DELETE: Removes resources.
  • HEAD: Fetches headers only (no body).
  • OPTIONS: Inspects allowed HTTP methods.

qs.q: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?
qs.a:
PUT vs. PATCH

  • PUT: Replaces the resource completely. Requires sending all resource attributes.
  • PATCH: Updates only the specific properties supplied in request.

qs.q: What does idempotent mean? Which HTTP methods are idempotent?
qs.a:
Idempotence

An operation is idempotent if executing it multiple times yields the exact same state and side-effects as executing it once.
Idempotent methods: GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS. POST is NOT idempotent.

qs.q: What is statelessness in REST?
qs.a:
Statelessness

Every request from client must contain all context and authentication tokens required to understand and process the request. No session history is saved on server memory.

qs.q: What is content negotiation?
qs.a:
Content Negotiation

The process where client and server negotiate output formats (e.g. JSON or XML) using headers: Accept (client requests format) and Content-Type (server returns format).

qs.q: What is HATEOAS?
qs.a:
HATEOAS

Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State. A REST constraint where API responses contain navigation links directing clients dynamically to next available actions.

qs.q: What are the 5 HTTP status code classes? Give 3 examples each.
qs.a:
HTTP Status Code Classes

  • 1xx (Informational): 100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols, 102 Processing.
  • 2xx (Success): 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content.
  • 3xx (Redirection): 301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found, 304 Not Modified.
  • 4xx (Client Error): 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden.
  • 5xx (Server Error): 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable.

qs.q: What is the difference between 200 OK and 201 Created?
qs.a:
200 OK vs. 201 Created

  • 200 OK: General success code returning request results (typically GET, PUT, PATCH updates).
  • 201 Created: Success code indicating that a new resource has been successfully created. Often returns resource URL in Location headers (typically POST).

qs.q: What is 204 No Content?
qs.a:
204 No Content

Request executed successfully but response body is intentionally empty (typically returned on DELETE requests).

qs.q: What is the difference between 400, 401, 403, and 404?
qs.a:
Client Error Codes

  • 400 Bad Request: Syntax error, missing params, invalid JSON body.
  • 401 Unauthorized: Client identity missing or unauthenticated.
  • 403 Forbidden: Client identity is known but lacks access privileges.
  • 404 Not Found: Target resource URL does not exist.

qs.q: What is 409 Conflict?
qs.a:
409 Conflict

Indicates that the request conflicts with database state (e.g. attempting to register a user email that already exists).

qs.q: What is 422 Unprocessable Entity?
qs.a:
422 Unprocessable Entity

Syntax is correct (valid JSON) but parameters contain validation errors (e.g. age value must be positive).

qs.q: What is 429 Too Many Requests?
qs.a:
429 Too Many Requests

The client has exceeded API rate limits within a specified time window.

qs.q: What is 500 vs 503?
qs.a:
500 vs. 503

  • 500 Internal Server Error: General unhandled server crash exception.
  • 503 Service Unavailable: Server is overloaded, down, or undergoing maintenance.

qs.q: What is API versioning? What are the common strategies?
qs.a:
API Versioning

Managing API changes without breaking existing clients.
Strategies: URI path (/v1/users), Request Parameter (/users?version=1), Custom Headers (X-API-Version: 1), Accept Header Media Type (Accept: application/vnd.company.v1+json).

qs.q: What is pagination in REST APIs? (offset vs cursor-based)
qs.a:
Pagination Strategies

  • Offset Pagination: Uses page/limit query inputs. Easy to map but slows down on high offsets and can skip/duplicate rows if records are updated.
  • Cursor-Based: Returns a pointer (ID/timestamp of last item). Highly performant, immune to concurrent data writes. Best for infinite scroll feeds.

qs.q: What is rate limiting and how is it implemented?
qs.a:
Rate Limiting

Throttling request limits per client to prevent API abuse. Implemented using Token Bucket or Leaky Bucket algorithms, often integrated into API Gateways or middleware using Redis counters.

qs.q: What is an API Gateway?
qs.a:
API Gateway

A single entry point for microservices that routes requests, handles authentication, applies rate limits, aggregates service outputs, and executes load balancing.

qs.q: What is Swagger/OpenAPI? Why is documentation important?
qs.a:
Swagger / OpenAPI

A standard specification language describing REST API endpoints, inputs, outputs, and security schemes, generating interactive UI docs (Swagger UI).

qs.q: What is CORS and how do you handle it in a Spring Boot app?
qs.a:
CORS in Spring Boot

Use the @CrossOrigin annotation on controllers, or configure a global WebMvcConfigurer bean mapping allowed origins and headers.

qs.q: What is an interceptor in Spring?
qs.a:
Interceptors

A class that intercepts controller requests before/after execution (implementing HandlerInterceptor) to log metrics, verify authentication tokens, or modify data models.

qs.q: What is idempotency key?
qs.a:
Idempotency Key

A unique UUID token sent by client in request headers (e.g. Idempotency-Key: ). The server caches this key; if a duplicate request is received, it returns the cached response, preventing duplicate operations.

qs.q: What is the difference between monolithic and microservices architecture?
qs.a:
Monolithic vs. Microservices

  • Monolithic: Entire system compiled and deployed as a single application package. Simple deployment, fast local calls, but hard to scale and slow to compile.
  • Microservices: System split into small, independently deployable services collaborating over network APIs. Scalable and resilient, but complex to coordinate.

qs.q: What are the advantages and disadvantages of microservices?
qs.a:
Microservices Trade-offs

Pros: Independent scaling, technology freedom, isolated fault scopes, fast team iterations.
Cons: Network overhead, complex transactions (requires Saga pattern), logging fragmentation, deployment orchestration.

qs.q: What is service discovery? (Eureka)
qs.a:
Service Discovery

A database directory tracking active microservice instance IP locations dynamically. Instances register with registry servers (like Netflix Eureka) at startup, and clients lookup paths dynamically to route traffic.

qs.q: What is a load balancer? Types?
qs.a:
Load Balancers

Distributes traffic across multiple backend instances.
Types: Hardware vs Software load balancers; Layer 4 (Transport layer TCP routing) vs Layer 7 (Application layer URL routing).

qs.q: What is a message queue? Difference between RabbitMQ and Kafka?
qs.a:
Message Queues

Systems enabling asynchronous, decoupled service communications.
  • RabbitMQ: Smart broker/dumb consumer model. Messages are routed, processed, and deleted immediately once consumed. Good for complex routing.
  • Apache Kafka: Distributed log model. Messages are persistent, structured in partitions. Consumers read messages from offsets. High throughput, good for event streaming.

qs.q: What is synchronous vs asynchronous communication in microservices?
qs.a:
Sync vs. Async

  • Synchronous: Caller blocks waiting for response (e.g. HTTP, gRPC). Tight coupling.
  • Asynchronous: Caller sends event message and continues immediately without blocking (e.g. RabbitMQ, Kafka). Loose coupling.

qs.q: What is the Circuit Breaker pattern? (Hystrix/Resilience4j)
qs.a:
Circuit Breakers

Prevents cascading microservice failures. If calls to a downstream service fail repeatedly, the breaker opens, immediately returning default fallback responses without overwhelming the failing service. Transitions: Closed -> Open -> Half-Open.

qs.q: What is an API gateway pattern?
qs.a:
API Gateway Pattern

Architectural design declaring a reverse proxy gateway routing API queries, ensuring backend services are hidden from public clients.

qs.q: What is caching? What are the different cache strategies? (write-through, write-behind, cache-aside)
qs.a:
Caching Strategies

  • Cache-Aside (Lazy loading): Application checks cache. If miss, queries DB, saves in cache, returns.
  • Write-Through: Application writes to cache, which writes to DB immediately before returning success.
  • Write-Behind (Write-Back): Application writes to cache, which updates DB asynchronously in batches. Fast writes, but potential data loss risk.

qs.q: What is Redis? What data structures does it support?
qs.a:
Redis

An in-memory key-value database, cache, and message broker.
Structures: Strings, Lists, Sets, Sorted Sets, Hashes, Bitmaps, HyperLogLogs, Geospatial indexes.

qs.q: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
qs.a:
SQL vs. NoSQL

SQL DatabasesNoSQL Databases
Relational table schemas, structured.Schema-less (Key-value, Document, Graph, Column).
Enforces ACID properties.Focuses on scaling and availability (BASE properties).
Vertical scaling.Horizontal scaling.

qs.q: When would you choose NoSQL over SQL?
qs.a:
Choosing NoSQL

Choose NoSQL when handling massive data volumes, schema layouts change rapidly, high write speeds are needed, or scaling horizontally across distributed nodes is a requirement.

qs.q: What is MongoDB? What is a document in MongoDB?
qs.a:
MongoDB

A document NoSQL database. A document is a JSON-like key-value format (BSON) representing a record (like a table row in SQL but with nested sub-structures).

qs.q: What is connection pooling and why does it matter?
qs.a:
Connection Pooling

Keeps database socket connections active for reuse, avoiding expensive TCP connection handshake overhead on every query.

qs.q: What is JWT? How does JWT-based authentication flow work?
qs.a:
JWT Authentication

1. Client logins with credentials.
2. Server validates login, signs a JWT token with key secrets, and returns it.
3. Client stores token (localStorage/cookie) and attaches it in Authorization headers.
4. Server validates signature on every request, extracting user details without database lookups.

qs.q: What is OAuth2? What are the grant types?
qs.a:
OAuth2 Grant Types

  • Authorization Code: Best for web server apps (most secure, uses authorization codes).
  • Client Credentials: Best for machine-to-machine service integration.
  • Implicit: Deprecated legacy flow for SPA web clients.
  • Password Credentials: Deprecated flow exchanging user password directly.

qs.q: What is the difference between session-based and token-based authentication?
qs.a:
Session vs. Token Auth

  • Session-Based: Stateful. Server stores session details in memory; client holds session ID in cookie. Hard to scale across distributed nodes.
  • Token-Based: Stateless. Server signs a self-contained token; client holds it. Server needs no session storage, easily scales horizontally.

qs.q: What is HTTPS? How does SSL/TLS handshake work?
qs.a:
HTTPS / TLS Handshake

Secure HTTP protocol using SSL/TLS encryption. Handshake steps:
1. Client Hello (supported cipher lists).
2. Server Hello + Certificate validation.
3. Key exchange (Client creates pre-master secret key encrypted using Server public key).
4. Session keys generated for fast symmetric encryption of data traffic.

qs.q: What is SQL injection? How do you prevent it?
qs.a:
SQL Injection Prevention

Attack execution where SQL queries are modified via malicious form inputs. Prevent using Parameterized Queries (PreparedStatement) and input sanitization.

qs.q: What is XSS and CSRF?
qs.a:
Web Vulnerabilities

  • XSS (Cross-Site Scripting): Malicious script injected into a webpage, executing on client browsers. Prevent using input escaping.
  • CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery): Tricking a logged-in user's browser into executing unauthorized actions on a web app. Prevent using CSRF anti-forgery tokens.

qs.q: What is the Singleton pattern? Thread-safe Singleton?
qs.a:
Singleton Pattern

Guarantees that a class has only one instance per JVM, providing a global access point.
Thread-safe implementation: Double-Checked Locking:
public class Singleton {
  private static volatile Singleton instance;
  private Singleton() {}
  public static Singleton getInstance() {
    if (instance == null) {
      synchronized (Singleton.class) {
        if (instance == null) instance = new Singleton();
      }
    }
    return instance;
  }
}

qs.q: What is the Factory pattern?
qs.a:
Factory Pattern

Creational pattern that abstracts object instantiation logic, returning concrete class instances based on input parameters.

qs.q: What is the Builder pattern? When is it better than telescoping constructors?
qs.a:
Builder Pattern

Creational pattern used to construct complex objects step-by-step. Better than telescoping constructors because it improves readability, handles optional parameters cleanly, and enforces object immutability.

qs.q: What is the Repository pattern?
qs.a:
Repository Pattern

Decouples domain business logic from the actual data persistence layer, providing a collection-like interface to access database records.

qs.q: What is the MVC pattern?
qs.a:
MVC Pattern

Splits UI applications into three distinct roles: Model (data), View (UI), and Controller (routing request dispatcher).

qs.q: What is the SOLID principle? Explain each letter.
qs.a:
SOLID Principles

  • S (Single Responsibility): A class should have only one reason to change.
  • O (Open/Closed): Open for extension, closed for modification.
  • L (Liskov Substitution): Subclasses must be substitutable for their parent classes.
  • I (Interface Segregation): Avoid fat interfaces; split them into small, specific interfaces.
  • D (Dependency Inversion): Depend on abstractions, not concretions.

qs.q: What is the DRY principle?
qs.a:
DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)

Every piece of knowledge or logic must have a single, unambiguous, authoritative representation within a system, reducing code duplication.

qs.q: What is the difference between coupling and cohesion?
qs.a:
Coupling vs. Cohesion

  • Cohesion: How closely focused the code/responsibilities inside a single class are. Best: High Cohesion.
  • Coupling: How dependent classes are on each other. Best: Low/Loose Coupling.

qs.q: What is Docker? What is the difference between a container and a VM?
qs.a:
Docker vs. Virtual Machines

  • Virtual Machine: Bundles application, libraries, and a complete Guest OS. Runs on a hypervisor, consuming heavy memory and boot time.
  • Docker Container: Shares host OS kernel, packaging only code and libraries. Extremely lightweight, boots in milliseconds.

qs.q: What is a Dockerfile? What is a Docker image vs container?
qs.a:
Docker Terminology

  • Dockerfile: A text script containing commands to build a Docker image.
  • Docker Image: A read-only snapshot template containing libraries and runtime configurations.
  • Docker Container: A runnable active instance of a Docker image.

qs.q: What is CI/CD? Name tools.
qs.a:
CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery)

Automates code compilation, testing, and deployment pipelines.
Tools: Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI.

qs.q: What is Maven? What is a POM file?
qs.a:
Maven & POM

A project management and build automation tool. The POM (Project Object Model - pom.xml) is the XML configuration file declaring dependencies, plug-ins, versions, and build tasks.

qs.q: What are the Maven lifecycle phases?
qs.a:
Maven Lifecycle Phases

Standard build steps executed sequentially: validate -> compile -> test -> package -> verify -> install -> deploy.

qs.q: What is Git? What is the difference between git merge and git rebase?
qs.a:
Git Branching Methods

  • git merge: Joins branch histories, creating a merge commit. Preserves exact history.
  • git rebase: Moves branch commits on top of another base, creating a flat linear commit history. Avoids merge commits, but rewrites history.

qs.q: What is a webhook?
qs.a:
Webhooks

An HTTP POST callback notification sent automatically by source systems to a target URL when events occur (e.g. notifications sent to Slack when build completes).

qs.q: What is the difference between vertical scaling and horizontal scaling?
qs.a:
Scaling Strategies

  • Vertical Scaling (Scale-up): Adding more resources (CPU/RAM) to a single database/server node. Has physical limits.
  • Horizontal Scaling (Scale-out): Adding more server nodes to cluster networks. Highly scalable.

qs.q: What is an idempotent operation in backend design?
qs.a:
Backend Idempotency

Design ensuring API requests can fail and retry safely (e.g. charge requests) without executing actions twice.

qs.q: Write a Java program to check if a number is even or odd using bitwise.
qs.a:
Even or Odd (Bitwise)

public static boolean isEven(int n) {
  return (n & 1) == 0;
}
Complexity: O(1) time, O(1) space.

qs.q: Write a Java program to reverse a number.
qs.a:
Reverse a Number

public static int reverse(int n) {
  int rev = 0;
  while(n != 0) {
    rev = rev * 10 + n % 10;
    n /= 10;
  }
  return rev;
}
Complexity: O(log10(N)) time, O(1) space.

qs.q: Check if a number is a palindrome.
qs.a:
Number Palindrome

public static boolean isPalindrome(int n) {
  if(n < 0) return false;
  int temp = n, rev = 0;
  while(temp != 0) {
    rev = rev * 10 + temp % 10;
    temp /= 10;
  }
  return n == rev;
}

qs.q: Find the sum of digits of a number.
qs.a:
Sum of Digits

public static int sumDigits(int n) {
  int sum = 0;
  while(n != 0) {
    sum += Math.abs(n % 10);
    n /= 10;
  }
  return sum;
}

qs.q: Check if a number is prime (optimized O(√n)).
qs.a:
Prime Check

public static boolean isPrime(int n) {
  if(n <= 1) return false;
  if(n <= 3) return true;
  if(n % 2 == 0 || n % 3 == 0) return false;
  for(int i = 5; i * i <= n; i += 6) {
    if(n % i == 0 || n % (i + 2) == 0) return false;
  }
  return true;
}

qs.q: Find the factorial of a number — iterative and recursive.
qs.a:
Factorial

// Iterative:
public static long factIter(int n) {
  long res = 1;
  for(int i=2; i<=n; i++) res *= i;
  return res;
}
// Recursive:
public static long factRec(int n) {
  return (n <= 1) ? 1 : n * factRec(n - 1);
}

qs.q: Print Fibonacci series — iterative and recursive.
qs.a:
Fibonacci Series

// Iterative:
public static void fibIter(int n) {
  int a=0, b=1;
  for(int i=0; i<n; i++) {
    System.out.print(a + " ");
    int next = a + b;
    a = b; b = next;
  }
}

qs.q: Check if a number is an Armstrong number.
qs.a:
Armstrong Number

public static boolean isArmstrong(int n) {
  int temp = n, sum = 0, digits = String.valueOf(n).length();
  while(temp != 0) {
    sum += Math.pow(temp % 10, digits);
    temp /= 10;
  }
  return sum == n;
}

qs.q: Find GCD of two numbers using Euclidean algorithm.
qs.a:
Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)

public static int gcd(int a, int b) {
  return (b == 0) ? a : gcd(b, a % b);
}

qs.q: Find LCM using GCD.
qs.a:
Lowest Common Multiple (LCM)

public static int lcm(int a, int b) {
  return (a * b) / gcd(a, b);
}

qs.q: Convert decimal to binary (manual method).
qs.a:
Decimal to Binary

public static String decToBin(int n) {
  StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
  while(n > 0) {
    sb.append(n % 2);
    n /= 2;
  }
  return sb.reverse().toString();
}

qs.q: Convert binary to decimal.
qs.a:
Binary to Decimal

public static int binToDec(String bin) {
  int dec = 0, power = 0;
  for(int i=bin.length()-1; i>=0; i--) {
    if(bin.charAt(i) == '1') {
      dec += Math.pow(2, power);
    }
    power++;
  }
  return dec;
}

qs.q: Count the number of set bits (1s) in binary representation.
qs.a:
Set Bits Count

public static int countSetBits(int n) {
  int count = 0;
  while(n > 0) {
    n = n & (n - 1); // Brian Kernighan's Algorithm
    count++;
  }
  return count;
}

qs.q: Swap two numbers without a temp variable.
qs.a:
Swap without Temp

// Using XOR:
a = a ^ b;
b = a ^ b;
a = a ^ b;

qs.q: Check if a number is a power of 2 using bitwise.
qs.a:
Power of 2 Check

public static boolean isPowerOfTwo(int n) {
  return n > 0 && (n & (n - 1)) == 0;
}

qs.q: Find all prime numbers up to N (Sieve of Eratosthenes).
qs.a:
Sieve of Eratosthenes

public static void sieve(int n) {
  boolean[] prime = new boolean[n+1];
  Arrays.fill(prime, true);
  for(int p=2; p*p<=n; p++) {
    if(prime[p]) {
      for(int i=p*p; i<=n; i+=p) prime[i] = false;
    }
  }
  for(int i=2; i<=n; i++) if(prime[i]) System.out.print(i + " ");
}

qs.q: Check if a number is a perfect number.
qs.a:
Perfect Number

public static boolean isPerfect(int n) {
  int sum = 1;
  for(int i=2; i*i<=n; i++) {
    if(n % i == 0) {
      sum += i;
      if(i * i != n) sum += n / i;
    }
  }
  return sum == n && n != 1;
}

qs.q: Reverse digits and check for integer overflow.
qs.a:
Safe Reverse with Overflow Check

public static int reverseSafe(int n) {
  int rev = 0;
  while(n != 0) {
    int tail = n % 10;
    int newRev = rev * 10 + tail;
    if((newRev - tail) / 10 != rev) return 0; // Overflow
    rev = newRev;
    n /= 10;
  }
  return rev;
}

qs.q: Two Sum — find a pair that adds up to a target.
qs.a:
Two Sum

public static int[] twoSum(int[] arr, int target) {
  Map map = new HashMap<>();
  for(int i=0; i<arr.length; i++) {
    int complement = target - arr[i];
    if(map.containsKey(complement)) return new int[]{map.get(complement), i};
    map.put(arr[i], i);
  }
  return new int[]{-1, -1};
}

qs.q: Maximum Subarray Sum — Kadane's Algorithm.
qs.a:
Kadane's Algorithm

public static int maxSubArray(int[] arr) {
  int maxSoFar = arr[0], currentMax = arr[0];
  for(int i=1; i<arr.length; i++) {
    currentMax = Math.max(arr[i], currentMax + arr[i]);
    maxSoFar = Math.max(maxSoFar, currentMax);
  }
  return maxSoFar;
}

qs.q: Implement binary search iteratively and recursively.
qs.a:
Binary Search

public static int binarySearch(int[] arr, int target) {
  int low = 0, high = arr.length - 1;
  while(low <= high) {
    int mid = low + (high - low)/2;
    if(arr[mid] == target) return mid;
    if(arr[mid] < target) low = mid + 1;
    else high = mid - 1;
  }
  return -1;
}

qs.q: Implement bubble sort — show the optimization.
qs.a:
Optimized Bubble Sort

for(int i=0; i<n-1; i++) {
  boolean swapped = false;
  for(int j=0; j<n-i-1; j++) {
    if(arr[j] > arr[j+1]) {
      swap(arr, j, j+1); swapped = true;
    }
  }

}

qs.q: Implement selection sort.
qs.a:
Selection Sort

for(int i=0; i<n-1; i++) {
  int minIdx = i;
  for(int j=i+1; j<n; j++) {
    if(arr[j] < arr[minIdx]) minIdx = j;
  }
  swap(arr, i, minIdx);
}

qs.q: Implement insertion sort.
qs.a:
Insertion Sort

for(int i=1; i<n; i++) {
  int key = arr[i], j = i - 1;
  while(j >= 0 && arr[j] > key) {
    arr[j+1] = arr[j]; j--;
  }
  arr[j+1] = key;
}

qs.q: Find the longest increasing subsequence length (basic DP).
qs.a:
Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS)

public static int lis(int[] arr) {
  int[] dp = new int[arr.length];
  Arrays.fill(dp, 1);
  int max = 1;
  for(int i=1; i<arr.length; i++) {
    for(int j=0; j<i; j++) {
      if(arr[i] > arr[j]) dp[i] = Math.max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1);
    }
    max = Math.max(max, dp[i]);
  }
  return max;
}

qs.q: Reverse a string in Java (without built-in reverse).

public static String reverse(String s) {
  char[] chars = s.toCharArray();

  while(l < r) {
    char temp = chars[l];
    chars[l++] = chars[r];
    chars[r--] = temp;
  }
  return new String(chars);
}

    if(s.charAt(l++) != s.charAt(r--)) return false;
  }
  return true;
}

  int[] counts = new int[256];
  for(int i=0; i<s1.length(); i++) {
    counts[s1.charAt(i)]++;
    counts[s2.charAt(i)]--;
  }
  for(int c : counts) if(c != 0) return false;
  return true;
}

qs.q: Find the longest substring without repeating characters.
qs.a:
Longest Substring (Sliding Window Map)

public static int lengthOfLongestSubstring(String s) {
  Map map = new HashMap<>();
  int max = 0, left = 0;
  for(int i=0; i<s.length(); i++) {
    if(map.containsKey(s.charAt(i))) left = Math.max(left, map.get(s.charAt(i)) + 1);
    map.put(s.charAt(i), i);
    max = Math.max(max, i - left + 1);
  }
  return max;
}

qs.q: What is the difference between HTML4 and HTML5? Key new features?
qs.a:
HTML4 vs. HTML5

  • Syntax: HTML5 has simplified doctypes (<!DOCTYPE html>) and encoding declarations.
  • Graphics: HTML5 adds native support for <canvas>, SVG, audio, and video elements.
  • APIs: Adds Geolocation, Web Storage (localStorage), and Web Workers.
  • Structure: Replaces layout divs with semantic structures (header, footer, nav, article).

qs.q: What are semantic HTML5 elements? Give 6 examples and why they matter.
qs.a:
Semantic HTML5

Elements that clearly describe their meaning to both browser and developer.
Examples: <header>, <nav>, <section>, <article>, <aside>, <footer>.
Importance: Crucial for SEO ranking, accessibility (screen readers), and clean code structure.

qs.q: What is the difference between <div> and <span>?
qs.a:
div vs. span

  • <div>: Block-level element. Starts on a new line, takes up full width available, and permits custom height/margins.
  • <span>: Inline-level element. Remains inline, takes up only required content width, and disregards height modifications.

qs.q: What is the difference between block-level and inline elements?
qs.a:
Block vs. Inline

Block elements (e.g. p, div, h1) start on new lines and fill full widths. Inline elements (e.g. a, span, img) display inline without line breaks.

qs.q: What are HTML data attributes? How do you access them in JS?
qs.a:
Data Attributes

Attributes starting with data- used to store custom metadata on elements.
Accessing in JS: Using the dataset property:
const val = element.dataset.userId; // accesses data-user-id

qs.q: What are meta tags? What does the viewport meta tag do?
qs.a:
Meta Tags & Viewport

Tags inside <head> supplying page metadata (author, descriptions, keywords). The **viewport meta tag** coordinates responsive mobile layouts:
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">

qs.q: What is the difference between href and src?
qs.a:
href vs. src

  • href: Hypertext Reference. Establishes links to resources (stylesheets, websites). Does not block rendering.
  • src: Source. Imports/embeds resources into the document (script files, images). Pauses rendering while loading.

qs.q: What is the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage?
qs.a:
Storage Comparison

  • localStorage: Saved indefinitely on client browser until cleared explicitly via script or cache wipe.
  • sessionStorage: Cleared automatically when the browser tab is closed.

qs.q: What are ARIA attributes? Why do they matter for accessibility?
qs.a:
ARIA Attributes

Accessible Rich Internet Applications attributes (e.g. aria-label, role="button") supplying metadata to screen readers to improve accessibility for disabled users.

qs.q: What is the difference between <script>, <script async>, and <script defer>?
qs.a:
Script Execution Modes

  • Standard: Pauses HTML parsing to download and run scripts immediately.
  • async: Downloads script in background, runs it the moment it finishes downloading, pausing HTML parsing. Non-blocking download, but execution order is not guaranteed.
  • defer: Downloads script in background. Runs script only after the HTML document is fully parsed. Execution order is guaranteed.

qs.q: What is the difference between <link> and <script> placement in HTML?
qs.a:
Resource Placement

Place <link rel="stylesheet"> in the <head> to render styles before content loads. Place <script> at the bottom of <body> (or use defer) to prevent scripts from blocking HTML parsing.

qs.q: What is a semantic form? What HTML5 input types exist?
qs.a:
Semantic Forms

Forms using inputs that define specific data schemas (e.g. email, url, date, tel, number), triggering automatic browser validation checks.

qs.q: What is the CSS Box Model? Explain each layer.
qs.a:
CSS Box Model

A design container wrapping every HTML element. Layers from inside out:
1. Content: The actual text/images.
2. Padding: Transparent space surrounding content inside borders.
3. Border: Line surrounding padding and content.
4. Margin: Transparent space separating the element from other elements.

qs.q: What is the difference between margin and padding?
qs.a:
Margin vs. Padding

  • Margin: Outer spacing between elements.
  • Padding: Inner spacing between content and border.

qs.q: What is the difference between position: static, relative, absolute, fixed, and sticky?
qs.a:
CSS Position Options

  • static (default): Flows standard document layout.
  • relative: Positioned relative to its normal layout flow. Supports top/left offsets without affecting other elements.
  • absolute: Positioned relative to its closest non-static parent element. Taken out of normal document flow.
  • fixed: Positioned relative to the browser viewport. Remains locked in place during scroll.
  • sticky: Hybrid. Behaves like relative until scroll threshold is reached, then locks in place like fixed.

qs.q: What is Flexbox? Name the key container and item properties.
qs.a:
Flexbox Layout

A one-dimensional layout model for organizing elements in rows or columns.
Container properties: display: flex, flex-direction, justify-content (main axis alignment), align-items (cross axis alignment).
Item properties: flex-grow, flex-shrink, flex-basis, align-self.

qs.q: What is CSS Grid? How is it different from Flexbox?
qs.a:
CSS Grid vs. Flexbox

  • Flexbox: One-dimensional (aligns elements in a single row OR column). Good for menu bars.
  • Grid: Two-dimensional (aligns elements in rows AND columns simultaneously). Good for page layouts.

qs.q: What is the difference between display: none and visibility: hidden?
qs.a:
Hiding Elements

  • display: none: Removes the element from the layout flow entirely. It takes up no space.
  • visibility: hidden: Hides the element, but the element still takes up its original space in the layout flow.

qs.q: What is CSS specificity? How is it calculated?
qs.a:
CSS Specificity

The weight hierarchy determining which styles apply if rules conflict.
Calculated as: Inline styles (1000) > ID selectors (100) > Classes, attributes, pseudo-classes (10) > Elements and pseudo-elements (1).

qs.q: What is z-index? What is a stacking context?
qs.a:
z-index & Stacking Context

z-index sets the vertical overlap priority of elements (requires non-static positioning). A **stacking context** is a conceptual group of elements moving together up/down the overlap layer.

qs.q: What is responsive design? How do media queries work?
qs.a:
Responsive Design

Design adaptively formatting across mobile, tablet, and desktop viewports. **Media queries** apply styles dynamically based on device queries:
@media (max-width: 768px) {
  .sidebar { display: none; }
}

qs.q: What is the difference between px, em, rem, vh, vw?
qs.a:
CSS Units

  • px: Absolute pixel units.
  • em: Relative to the font-size of the parent element.
  • rem: Relative to the font-size of the root element (<html>).
  • vh/vw: Percentage units relative to 1% of viewport height/width.

qs.q: What is a CSS pseudo-class vs a pseudo-element? Give examples.
qs.a:
Pseudo selectors

  • Pseudo-class: Targets element states. Example: :hover, :focus, :nth-child().
  • Pseudo-element: Targets virtual parts of elements. Example: ::before, ::after, ::first-letter.

qs.q: What is a CSS variable (custom property)? Syntax?
qs.a:
CSS Variables

Allows defining reusable style tokens dynamically.
Syntax:
:root {
  --main-color: #4f8ef7;
}
.btn { color: var(--main-color); }

qs.q: What is the difference between var, let, and const?
qs.a:
var vs. let vs. const

Featurevarletconst
ScopeFunction scopeBlock scopeBlock scope
HoistingHoisted (initialized to undefined)Hoisted (in Temporal Dead Zone)Hoisted (in TDZ)
ReassignYesYesNo

qs.q: What is hoisting in JavaScript? Does let get hoisted?
qs.a:
JavaScript Hoisting

Behavior where variable and function declarations are moved to the top of their scope during compilation.
Does let/const get hoisted? Yes, but they are not initialized. They reside in the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) until execution reaches their declaration line, throwing a ReferenceError if accessed early.

qs.q: What is a closure? Give a real-world use case.
qs.a:
Closures

A function that retains references to variables declared in its lexical scope environment, even when executed outside that scope.
Use case: Implementing private variables/methods (encapsulation).
function counter() {
  let count = 0;
  return () => ++count;
}
const inc = counter(); inc(); // returns 1

qs.q: What is the event loop? Explain the call stack, Web APIs, and callback queue.
qs.a:
The Event Loop

A mechanism enabling JS to run asynchronous tasks concurrently on a single thread.
  • Call Stack: Executes synchronous code frames.
  • Web APIs: Browser container managing async threads (timeouts, fetches).
  • Callback (Task) Queue: Holds callbacks ready to run.
  • Event Loop: Monitors the stack. When stack is empty, it pushes the first task from the callback queue onto the stack.

qs.q: What is the difference between == and ===?
qs.a:
== vs. ===

  • ==: Loose equality. Compares values after performing type coercion.
  • ===: Strict equality. Compares both values AND types without coercion.

qs.q: What is the difference between null and undefined?
qs.a:
null vs. undefined

  • undefined: Variable declared but holds no assigned value. Set automatically by JavaScript.
  • null: Intentional assignment representing the empty or non-existent value.

qs.q: What does typeof null return and why?
qs.a:
typeof null

Returns "object". This is a legacy design bug in the original JavaScript implementation where variables were represented as type tags (objects shared tag 000, and null represented empty pointer which mapped to 000).

qs.q: What is prototypal inheritance?
qs.a:
Prototypal Inheritance

The inheritance mechanism in JS. Every object has an internal link pointer (prototype) linking to other parent objects. Methods are inherited down this prototype chain.

qs.q: What is the difference between function declaration and function expression?
qs.a:
Function Declarations vs. Expressions

  • Declaration: function foo() {}. Hoisted completely, can be called before declaration line.
  • Expression: const foo = function() {}. Variable is hoisted, but function assignment is not. Cannot be called before assignment.

qs.q: What is an arrow function? How is this different inside it?
qs.a:
Arrow Functions

Short function syntax (() => {}). Arrow functions do not declare their own this context; instead, they bind this lexically, inheriting it from the parent enclosing scope.

qs.q: What is the this keyword? How does its value change in different contexts?
qs.a:
this Keyword Contexts

Refers to the execution context object.
  • Global scope: window (in browsers).
  • Method call: refers to the object calling the method.
  • Arrow functions: inherited lexically from enclosing parent scope.
  • Event handlers: refers to the DOM element receiving the event.

qs.q: What is the difference between call(), apply(), and bind()?
qs.a:
call() vs. apply() vs. bind()

  • call(obj, arg1, arg2): Executes function immediately, binding this to obj, passing arguments individually.
  • apply(obj, [args]): Executes function immediately, binding this to obj, passing arguments as an array.
  • bind(obj): Returns a new function instance with this permanently bound to obj, to be executed later.

qs.q: What is a callback function? What is callback hell?
qs.a:
Callbacks

A function passed as an argument to another function to execute after asynchronous operations complete. **Callback Hell** is nested asynchronous callbacks, resulting in hard-to-read 'pyramid of doom' code.

qs.q: What is a Promise? What are its states?
qs.a:
Promises

An object representing the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation.
States: pending (initial state), fulfilled (operation succeeded), rejected (operation failed).

qs.q: What is async/await? How does it relate to Promises?
qs.a:
async/await

Syntactic sugar built on top of JavaScript Promises, making asynchronous code look and behave like synchronous code.

qs.q: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous JS?
qs.a:
Sync vs. Async JS

Synchronous executes blocking statements in sequence line-by-line. Asynchronous delegates tasks (fetches, timeouts) to execution containers, allowing main threads to continue processing UI frames.

qs.q: What is destructuring? Show object and array destructuring.
qs.a:
Destructuring

Unpacking values from arrays or objects directly into variables:
// Object:
const {name, age} = user;
// Array:
const [first, second] = arr;

qs.q: What is the spread operator vs rest parameter?
qs.a:
Spread vs. Rest (...)

  • Spread: Expands iterable elements (e.g. [...arr1, ...arr2]).
  • Rest: Gathers multiple arguments into a single array (e.g. function run(...args) {}).

qs.q: What is the difference between map(), filter(), and reduce()?
qs.a:
Array Utilities

  • map(): Transforms array elements, returning a new array of same size.
  • filter(): Selects elements matching conditions, returning a filtered array.
  • reduce(): Aggregates elements into a single output value.

qs.q: What is event bubbling and event capturing?
qs.a:
Event Propagation

  • Event Capturing: Event starts at document root, propagating down to the target element.
  • Event Bubbling (default): Event starts at the target element, propagating up the DOM tree to document root.

qs.q: What is event delegation?
qs.a:
Event Delegation

Adding a single event listener to a parent element to manage events for all children (present or future) by inspecting event.target.

qs.q: What is the difference between localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies?
qs.a:
Storage Comparison

FeaturelocalStoragesessionStorageCookies
Capacity~5MB - 10MB~5MB~4KB
ExpiryNeverTab closeSet manually
Sent in requestNoNoYes (automatic)

qs.q: What is the fetch API? How do you handle errors with it?
qs.a:
Fetch API

Modern interface to make network requests. Note: fetch does not reject on HTTP error codes (like 404 or 500). You must check response status manually:
fetch(url)
  .then(res => {
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error('API Error');
    return res.json();
  });

qs.q: What is JSON.parse() and JSON.stringify()?
qs.a:
JSON Utilities

  • JSON.parse(): Converts a JSON string into a JavaScript object.
  • JSON.stringify(): Converts a JavaScript object into a JSON string.

qs.q: What are ES Modules? (import/export)
qs.a:
ES Modules

Standard modular design in JS. Enables importing/exporting variables, functions, or classes across script files using import and export statements.
